awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, you are beginning to understand what school vision means and how to communicate it. A school vision is a clear statement about the future the school is working towards. It gives direction to teachers, students, and families. At this level, you learn what your school vision says and why it was created. You begin to connect daily decisions in your team or department to the larger school direction. You also start to notice how your language — in meetings, emails, and conversations — can either support or weaken the vision. According to PSEL Standard 1, effective leaders develop and communicate a shared mission and vision. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 also emphasises that leaders must be able to articulate a compelling vision. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies vision articulation as a core managerial competency for school leaders. At this stage, your goal is not to create the vision, but to understand it deeply and begin using it in your communication.
keyTakeaways: Know your school vision and be able to explain it clearly to others.
Connect everyday decisions in your department to the school vision.
Use vision language consistently in meetings and written communication.
selfAssessment: I can explain the school vision in my own words to teachers in my department.
I can identify two or three ways my department's work connects to the school vision.
I can describe what PSEL Standard 1 says about mission and vision in school leadership.
I can give an example of how I have used vision language in a team meeting or written communication.
I can explain why communicating a clear vision is important for school improvement.

activity.task: Review your school's vision statement and prepare a short presentation for your department that connects the vision to your current department goals.
activity.output: A slide presentation (5–8 slides) showing the school vision, department goals, and the connection between them.
activity.evidence: Completed presentation file; meeting agenda or notes from the department session where it was shared.

practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, you communicate the school vision consistently within your team or department. You use the vision to guide planning and decision-making. You help teachers understand how their work connects to the school's direction. You gather feedback from your team about how well the vision is understood and acted upon. You also model vision-led language in meetings, appraisals, and professional conversations. PSEL Standard 1 states that leaders must build shared understanding of mission and vision across the school community. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 calls on leaders to actively promote the vision with all stakeholders. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 requires school leaders to demonstrate managerial competency in vision communication at all levels. At this stage, you move beyond understanding the vision yourself — you help others understand and own it. You use data or feedback to check if vision communication is working in your department.
keyTakeaways: Use the school vision actively in team planning, meetings, and professional conversations.
Help teachers see a clear connection between their daily work and the school's direction.
Gather simple feedback to check how well the vision is understood across your team.
selfAssessment: I can lead a department meeting where the school vision is used to guide planning decisions.
I can explain how my department's current priorities align with the school vision, using specific examples.
I can collect feedback from my team about how clearly the vision is communicated and understood.
I can use vision-aligned language consistently when giving feedback to teachers.
I can identify gaps between the stated vision and current departmental practice, and plan steps to address them.

activity.task: Design a department communication plan that embeds the school vision into regular team activities for one term.
activity.output: A written communication plan (1–2 pages) showing how vision language and goals will be integrated into team meetings, planning sessions, and appraisal conversations.
activity.evidence: Completed communication plan; example meeting agenda or appraisal notes that demonstrate vision-aligned language.

advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, you lead vision communication across a programme or phase. You work with multiple teams to build a consistent understanding of the school vision. You facilitate whole-school conversations about what the vision means in practice. You use critical reflection to evaluate whether vision communication is effective, and you adjust your approach based on evidence. You may redesign how the vision is embedded in school documents, planning tools, or professional development activities. PSEL Standard 1 emphasises that leaders build and sustain shared understanding of vision across the whole school community. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 requires leaders to communicate the vision convincingly to diverse stakeholders. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies vision articulation as central to the principal's managerial role across all school functions. At this level, your leadership helps create a shared language around vision that extends beyond your own department.
keyTakeaways: Facilitate whole-school or cross-departmental conversations about the meaning and application of the school vision.
Use evidence and reflection to evaluate the effectiveness of vision communication across programmes or phases.
Redesign school documents or practices to embed vision language more consistently.
selfAssessment: I can facilitate a whole-school or cross-departmental workshop focused on deepening understanding of the school vision.
I can critically analyse data or feedback to evaluate whether the vision is understood and acted upon across multiple departments.
I can revise school planning documents or templates to embed vision language more explicitly.
I can coach other leaders to communicate the school vision more effectively with their teams.
I can produce a structured report for senior leadership on the current state of vision communication across programmes or phases.

activity.task: Conduct a vision communication audit across at least two departments or phases, using teacher surveys or meeting observations as data sources.
activity.output: A written audit report (2–3 pages) with findings, analysis, and three or more recommendations to strengthen vision communication school-wide.
activity.evidence: Completed audit report; supporting data such as survey results or observation notes; record of conversations with department heads.

lead.reading: At the Lead/Mentor level, you shape how vision communication works across the whole school. You design school-wide systems and structures that keep the vision alive in daily practice. You mentor other leaders to develop their capacity to articulate and communicate vision. You represent the school vision to external stakeholders — including Cambridge networks, community partners, and regulatory bodies. You also lead processes to review and refresh the vision when the school context changes. PSEL Standard 1 calls on lead leaders to sustain a shared mission and vision that drives improvement over time. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 emphasises that the most effective leaders create conditions where everyone in the school community understands and owns the vision. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 places responsibility on the principal to lead vision articulation as a whole-school competency. At this level, your influence extends beyond your school to the wider professional community.
keyTakeaways: Design and maintain school-wide systems that keep the vision embedded in all major decisions and communications.
Mentor other leaders to develop their ability to communicate vision confidently and consistently.
Represent and advocate for your school vision to external stakeholders and professional networks.
selfAssessment: I can design a whole-school communication framework that embeds the vision in all key structures, from governance to classroom practice.
I can mentor at least two other school leaders to improve their vision communication skills, using coaching conversations and structured feedback.
I can present the school vision persuasively to external audiences, including Cambridge centre meetings, parent forums, or regulatory inspections.
I can lead a process to review and update the school vision when the school's context or strategic direction changes.
I can evaluate the long-term effectiveness of vision communication by analysing trends in staff, student, and community feedback over time.

activity.task: Design and lead a school-wide vision refresh process, including consultation with staff, students, families, and external partners.
activity.output: A vision communication strategy document (3–5 pages) that includes revised messaging, a stakeholder engagement plan, and a monitoring framework for the next two years.
activity.evidence: Final strategy document; evidence of stakeholder consultation such as meeting records or survey summaries; mentoring logs with other leaders involved in the process.

awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, you are beginning to understand what school improvement planning means and how monitoring works. A school improvement plan sets out the school's priorities, actions, and targets for a period — usually one year. At this level, you learn how your school creates and uses its improvement plan. You begin to understand how to set clear goals with measurable indicators. You also start to see how monitoring data — such as student results, lesson observations, or staff feedback — connects to the priorities in the plan. PSEL Standard 10 emphasises that effective leaders use data and evidence to drive school improvement. Cambridge School Leader Standard 2 states that leaders must develop and implement improvement plans that are evidence-based. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies school improvement planning as a core managerial competency for principals. At this stage, your focus is on understanding the structure and purpose of improvement planning, not on leading the whole process yourself.
keyTakeaways: Learn the structure of your school's improvement plan and how its priorities were identified.
Understand how measurable targets and success indicators are set in improvement planning.
Begin connecting monitoring data from your department to the goals in the school plan.
selfAssessment: I can explain the structure and main priorities of my school's current improvement plan.
I can identify the success indicators used in the school improvement plan and explain what they measure.
I can describe how monitoring data from my department connects to at least one priority in the school improvement plan.
I can explain what PSEL Standard 10 says about using evidence to support school improvement.
I can identify one area in my department where data suggests improvement is needed, and link this to the school improvement plan.

activity.task: Review your school's current improvement plan and prepare a brief written summary for your department that explains the plan's priorities and how your team's work connects to them.
activity.output: A one-page written summary with three sections: key school improvement priorities, department-level connections, and one monitoring action your team can take this term.
activity.evidence: Completed summary document; record of the team discussion where it was shared.

practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, you apply school improvement planning consistently within your team or department. You help set priorities, write clear goals, and identify success indicators for your area. You lead regular monitoring activities — such as reviewing student data, observing lessons, or analysing feedback — and use the results to adjust your department's actions. You communicate progress to your team in a structured and honest way. PSEL Standard 10 calls on leaders to use a continuous improvement process, including planning, monitoring, and reflective adjustments. Cambridge School Leader Standard 2 requires leaders to implement plans with clear actions and regular review points. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 includes monitoring and evaluation as key responsibilities of the school principal. At this stage, improvement planning is not a one-time event — it is a regular cycle you lead in your department, with clear evidence of impact.
keyTakeaways: Lead a regular planning and monitoring cycle in your department, with clear goals and evidence checkpoints.
Use data from multiple sources — student results, observations, surveys — to inform your department's improvement actions.
Communicate progress honestly and regularly to your team, adjusting plans when the evidence shows it is needed.
selfAssessment: I can develop a department improvement plan that includes SMART goals, actions, and success indicators aligned to school priorities.
I can lead a monitoring review meeting where data is discussed and decisions are made about next steps.
I can use student performance data, lesson observation findings, and teacher feedback to identify trends and adjust the department plan.
I can write a concise progress report on department improvement that is suitable for sharing with school leadership.
I can explain to my team how their individual contributions connect to the department improvement goals.

activity.task: Develop a department improvement plan for one term, lead two monitoring review meetings using evidence, and produce an end-of-term progress report.
activity.output: A department improvement plan (1–2 pages) and a written end-of-term progress report (1 page) with a summary of data reviewed and adjustments made.
activity.evidence: Completed improvement plan; meeting records from two monitoring reviews; end-of-term progress report shared with school leadership.

advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, you lead school improvement planning across a programme or phase. You work with multiple department heads to ensure their plans are aligned to school-wide priorities. You analyse complex data sets — including Cambridge results, stakeholder surveys, and external benchmarks — to evaluate overall improvement progress. You use critical reflection to assess whether the school's improvement strategies are working, and you report findings clearly to senior leaders. PSEL Standard 10 requires leaders to use data systems to continuously evaluate and improve teaching and learning at scale. Cambridge School Leader Standard 2 emphasises that leaders at this stage must manage the whole improvement cycle, from planning to impact evaluation. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 places whole-school improvement planning oversight as a principal-level responsibility. At this level, your role is to build the capacity of other leaders to plan and monitor improvement effectively.
keyTakeaways: Align improvement plans across multiple departments or phases to the whole-school improvement priorities.
Analyse complex data to evaluate whether school improvement strategies are producing measurable impact.
Build the capacity of other leaders to plan, monitor, and report on improvement in their own areas.
selfAssessment: I can facilitate a process where department heads align their improvement plans to whole-school priorities.
I can analyse data from multiple departments to identify cross-school trends and report findings to senior leadership.
I can evaluate the effectiveness of the school's improvement strategies using Cambridge results, benchmarking data, and stakeholder feedback.
I can coach department heads to write better improvement plans with measurable indicators and realistic timelines.
I can produce a structured mid-year improvement review report for the school principal or board, with evidence-based recommendations.

activity.task: Lead a mid-year school improvement review across at least three departments. Collect data, facilitate department leader discussions, and produce a whole-school review report.
activity.output: A written mid-year improvement review report (3–4 pages) covering: data summary, analysis of progress against targets, key challenges, and recommendations for the second half of the year.
activity.evidence: Completed review report; meeting records with department heads; evidence of data used such as tables or charts; record of report shared with senior leadership.

lead.reading: At the Lead/Mentor level, you design and steward the school's whole improvement planning system. You create the frameworks, tools, and processes that other leaders use to plan, monitor, and report on improvement. You mentor school leaders at all levels to develop stronger improvement planning skills. You ensure that the improvement plan is connected to the school's vision, Cambridge accreditation requirements, and Indonesian regulatory obligations. You also represent the school's improvement work to external bodies — including Cambridge, accreditation teams, and government inspectors. PSEL Standard 10 calls on lead leaders to build a culture of continuous improvement with strong systems that are self-sustaining. Cambridge School Leader Standard 2 requires the most effective leaders to embed improvement planning as a structural feature of school governance. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 places ultimate accountability for school improvement on the school principal. At this level, you build systems — not just plans.
keyTakeaways: Design the school-wide improvement planning framework, including templates, timelines, and reporting structures used by all leaders.
Ensure improvement planning is connected to Cambridge accreditation criteria and Indonesian regulatory requirements.
Mentor other leaders to develop strong improvement planning capacity, and measure the effectiveness of their growth over time.
selfAssessment: I can design a whole-school improvement planning framework with clear templates, processes, and reporting cycles for all leadership levels.
I can connect the school improvement plan explicitly to Cambridge School Evaluation Framework criteria and Permendikbud regulatory requirements.
I can present the school's improvement work persuasively to Cambridge inspectors, accreditation teams, or government representatives.
I can mentor two or more school leaders to develop their improvement planning skills, using structured coaching and review conversations.
I can evaluate the effectiveness of the school's improvement planning system over time and make changes to strengthen it.

activity.task: Design or significantly revise the school's whole improvement planning framework. Pilot it with two department heads and refine based on their feedback.
activity.output: A complete improvement planning framework document (4–6 pages) including: planning templates, monitoring schedule, reporting guidelines, and a guide for department heads.
activity.evidence: Final framework document; feedback records from the pilot with department heads; evidence of framework adoption in school leadership meetings; mentoring logs.

advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, you lead your school's engagement with the Cambridge school evaluation and self-review process. Cambridge schools are expected to use the Cambridge School Evaluation Framework to review their own performance and plan for improvement. At this level, you coordinate self-review activities — including gathering evidence, facilitating evaluation meetings, and producing structured self-evaluation documents. You analyse Cambridge results data, lesson observation findings, and stakeholder surveys to form honest judgements about school quality. You ensure that self-review is not a compliance exercise — it is a genuine tool for improvement. Cambridge School Leader Standard 3 emphasises that leaders must conduct rigorous and evidence-based self-review. PSEL Standard 10 calls on leaders to use data and evaluation systematically to support improvement. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 includes evaluation and reporting as a key managerial competency for principals. At this stage, you help your school see itself clearly and act on what it finds.
keyTakeaways: Lead the school's self-review process using the Cambridge School Evaluation Framework as the guiding structure.
Gather and analyse multiple sources of evidence — results data, observations, surveys — to form honest evaluation judgements.
Ensure self-review findings are documented clearly and used to inform the school improvement plan.
selfAssessment: I can lead a structured self-review process using the Cambridge School Evaluation Framework, involving staff from multiple departments.
I can analyse Cambridge examination results and benchmarking data to make evidence-based judgements about the school's academic performance.
I can facilitate a self-evaluation meeting where leaders discuss findings honestly and agree on priorities for improvement.
I can write a school self-evaluation document that summarises findings, strengths, and development areas in clear, structured language.
I can connect the conclusions of the self-review directly to the school improvement plan, ensuring priorities are acted upon.

activity.task: Lead a full self-review cycle for one domain of the Cambridge School Evaluation Framework. Gather evidence, facilitate a leadership discussion, and write a self-evaluation report.
activity.output: A written self-evaluation report (3–4 pages) for one SEF domain, including: evidence summary, evaluation judgement, identified strengths, and areas for development.
activity.evidence: Completed self-evaluation report; evidence log listing data sources used; record of the leadership evaluation meeting; link to school improvement plan priorities.

lead.reading: At the Lead/Mentor level, you design and lead the school's entire self-review system. You create the processes, tools, and culture that make self-review a regular and honest activity — not just a preparation exercise for external inspections. You mentor other leaders to develop the skills needed to evaluate their own areas critically and accurately. You ensure that self-review findings are shared with the school community — including staff, families, and the Cambridge centre — in a transparent and constructive way. You also represent the school's self-evaluation work during formal Cambridge school visits or accreditation processes. Cambridge School Leader Standard 3 requires the most effective leaders to build a self-review culture where honesty and evidence drive all improvement decisions. PSEL Standard 10 calls on lead leaders to establish data-informed evaluation as a core part of school governance. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 places accountability for school evaluation with the principal. At this level, self-review becomes part of how the school operates every day.
keyTakeaways: Build a whole-school culture of honest self-review where evidence — not assumption — drives improvement decisions.
Mentor other leaders to lead self-evaluation in their own areas using structured frameworks and evidence.
Represent the school's self-review work confidently to Cambridge and external bodies during formal evaluation processes.
selfAssessment: I can design a school-wide self-review system with a clear annual calendar, evidence protocols, and reporting formats for all leadership levels.
I can mentor other leaders to conduct self-evaluation in their own areas using the Cambridge School Evaluation Framework or equivalent structures.
I can lead the preparation and presentation of the school's self-evaluation during formal Cambridge school visits or external reviews.
I can analyse self-review data across the whole school to identify systemic patterns and present findings to the school board or governing body.
I can evaluate the quality and honesty of the school's self-review culture over time and make adjustments to the system when needed.

activity.task: Design or significantly improve the school's annual self-review calendar and evidence system. Present the system to school leaders and train two leaders to use it.
activity.output: A self-review system document (4–5 pages) including: annual review calendar, evidence collection protocols, guidance for department-level self-evaluation, and a reporting template for Cambridge.
activity.evidence: Final system document; training session records; evidence of system use such as completed self-evaluation sections; feedback from leaders trained.

advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, you lead planned change initiatives at programme or phase level. Leading change in a school is complex. It requires understanding why change is needed, engaging stakeholders in the process, and managing the human side of change — including uncertainty and resistance. At this level, you design and lead a structured change process with clear communication, support systems, and review points. You reflect critically on what is working and adjust your approach when challenges arise. You ensure that change does not lose momentum after the initial launch. PSEL Standard 6 calls on leaders to improve the organisation by building a culture of professional practice and change capacity. Cambridge School Leader Standard 4 emphasises that effective leaders manage change strategically, with attention to people as well as processes. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies the ability to lead institutional change as a core managerial competency for principals. At this stage, you move from implementing other people's change decisions to leading your own change initiatives.
keyTakeaways: Design a structured change plan with clear rationale, stakeholder engagement strategy, communication timeline, and review points.
Manage the human side of change by identifying potential resistance and creating support systems for staff.
Reflect critically on progress at each stage and adjust the change strategy when evidence shows it is needed.
selfAssessment: I can develop a change management plan for a specific school initiative, including rationale, stakeholder mapping, communication strategy, and timeline.
I can lead consultation sessions with staff and other leaders to build shared understanding and support for a planned change.
I can identify sources of resistance to a change initiative and design appropriate responses that maintain trust and momentum.
I can monitor the implementation of a change initiative using clear indicators, and adjust the plan when review data shows it is needed.
I can write a critical reflection on a completed change initiative, evaluating what worked, what did not, and what I would do differently.

activity.task: Lead a change initiative at programme or phase level — for example, introducing a new assessment approach, restructuring a curriculum programme, or launching a new professional learning model. Plan, implement, and review one phase of the initiative.
activity.output: A change initiative report (3–4 pages) covering: context and rationale, stakeholder engagement process, implementation summary, monitoring data, and critical reflection on progress.
activity.evidence: Completed change initiative report; stakeholder consultation records; monitoring data; reflection notes or journal entries from the implementation period.

lead.reading: At the Lead/Mentor level, you create the conditions for sustained improvement and continuous change capacity across the whole school. You design the structures and culture that allow your school to manage change well — not just for one initiative, but as an ongoing capability. You mentor other leaders to plan and lead change in their own areas, helping them build the skills and confidence to do this independently. You also evaluate whether past changes have truly improved the school, and you ensure that successful innovations are embedded into the school's permanent systems. PSEL Standard 6 requires lead leaders to establish organisations where improvement is continuous and self-sustaining. Cambridge School Leader Standard 4 calls on the most effective leaders to embed change management capacity into the school's leadership culture. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 places responsibility for institutional sustainability with the school principal. At this level, the school can manage change effectively even when you are not leading it directly.
keyTakeaways: Build whole-school capacity for change management, so that multiple leaders can lead and sustain improvement initiatives independently.
Mentor other leaders to develop change leadership skills through structured coaching and reflective practice.
Evaluate whether past changes have produced lasting improvement, and embed successful innovations into school systems and culture.
selfAssessment: I can design a whole-school change management framework that supports leaders at all levels to plan and lead initiatives systematically.
I can mentor two or more school leaders to develop their change leadership skills, using a structured coaching approach with documented progress.
I can evaluate the long-term impact of major change initiatives by analysing data across multiple years and presenting findings to the school board.
I can identify change initiatives that have been successful and design a process to embed them permanently into school policy and practice.
I can represent the school's improvement journey and change leadership to external stakeholders, including Cambridge networks, accreditation bodies, and government inspectors.

activity.task: Conduct a long-term impact review of two or more major change initiatives your school has implemented in the past three years. Lead mentor conversations with two school leaders about their own change projects.
activity.output: A long-term impact review document (4–6 pages) summarising: initiative background, evidence of impact, lessons learned, and recommendations for embedding successful changes into school systems. Include a mentoring log showing two structured coaching sessions.
activity.evidence: Completed impact review document; mentoring logs; evidence of data used in the review; record of report shared with senior leadership or board.

awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, you are beginning to understand what curriculum review means and why vertical coherence matters. Vertical coherence means that learning builds logically from one year group to the next, so students are well prepared for each stage of their education. At this level, you learn how your school's curriculum is structured across year groups in your subject or department. You begin to identify where there are gaps or overlaps in what students are learning. You also start to understand how Cambridge curriculum frameworks provide guidance on what students should learn and in what order. PSEL Standard 4 states that effective leaders develop and support intellectually rigorous and coherent curricula. Cambridge School Leader Standard 2 requires leaders to ensure that teaching and learning are purposeful and well organised across the school. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies curriculum management as a core managerial competency for school principals. At this stage, your focus is on understanding how curriculum is organised and beginning to notice where coherence may be missing.
keyTakeaways: Understand how the Cambridge curriculum framework structures learning progression from one year group to the next.
Identify where gaps or overlaps exist in your subject or department's curriculum map.
Use curriculum documents — such as scope and sequence maps and syllabuses — to review what is taught and when.
selfAssessment: I can explain what vertical coherence means and why it is important for student learning.
I can describe how the Cambridge curriculum framework organises learning progression in my subject area.
I can identify at least two areas in my department's curriculum where there are gaps or overlaps between year groups.
I can explain what PSEL Standard 4 says about intellectually rigorous and coherent curriculum.
I can read a curriculum map or scheme of work and identify where vertical alignment may need improvement.

activity.task: Review the curriculum map or schemes of work for your subject or department across at least three year groups, and prepare a written analysis identifying areas of strength and areas that need better alignment.
activity.output: A written curriculum alignment review (1–2 pages) showing: what is taught in each year group, where progression is clear, and where gaps or overlaps exist.
activity.evidence: Completed curriculum alignment review document; list of curriculum documents reviewed; record of discussion with at least one colleague about the findings.

practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, you lead curriculum review and promote vertical coherence within your team or department. You work with teachers to examine what is taught across year groups and ensure that learning builds logically from one stage to the next. You use Cambridge curriculum frameworks, past examination syllabuses, and internal assessment data to guide the review process. You facilitate collaborative planning sessions where teachers discuss how their units connect with what students learned in previous years and what they will need to know in future years. You also document curriculum decisions clearly so that they can be reviewed and updated over time. PSEL Standard 4 calls on leaders to ensure that curriculum is coherent, rigorous, and aligned to the school's academic goals. Cambridge School Leader Standard 2 requires leaders to lead the planning and delivery of a well-structured programme of study. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies curriculum management as a key managerial responsibility of the principal. At this stage, curriculum review is a regular, structured activity you lead — not a one-time task.
keyTakeaways: Lead structured curriculum review sessions where teachers examine learning progression across year groups.
Use Cambridge frameworks and examination syllabuses to check that curriculum content is appropriately sequenced.
Document curriculum decisions clearly so the department has a consistent and up-to-date curriculum map.
selfAssessment: I can facilitate a curriculum review meeting where teachers examine vertical alignment across year groups.
I can use Cambridge examination syllabuses and curriculum frameworks to check that content is taught in the right sequence.
I can identify and address gaps or overlaps in the department curriculum map, documenting the changes made.
I can produce or update a department curriculum map that shows the scope and sequence of learning across all year groups.
I can explain to teachers why vertical coherence matters and how it supports student achievement in Cambridge programmes.

activity.task: Lead a department curriculum review for one Cambridge subject, focusing on vertical coherence across at least four year groups. Update the department curriculum map based on the findings.
activity.output: An updated curriculum map showing the scope and sequence across all year groups, with a short written explanation (1 page) of the key changes made and the rationale for each change.
activity.evidence: Updated curriculum map; written explanation of changes; record of collaborative review meetings with department teachers.

advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, you lead curriculum review across a programme or phase, working with multiple subject departments to ensure both vertical and horizontal coherence. Horizontal coherence means that the curriculum across different subjects at the same year level is logically connected and not unnecessarily repetitive. At this level, you design and facilitate curriculum review processes that involve multiple departments. You analyse curriculum data — including Cambridge syllabus updates, internal assessments, and external benchmarks — to evaluate overall curriculum quality. You challenge departments to think critically about what they teach, why they teach it, and how it connects to the school's academic goals. PSEL Standard 4 calls on leaders to ensure the whole school's curriculum is coherent, rigorous, and equitable. Cambridge School Leader Standard 2 requires leaders at this level to take responsibility for the quality of learning programmes across the school. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 includes curriculum oversight as a principal-level responsibility. At this level, curriculum quality is a shared leadership responsibility you actively build.
keyTakeaways: Lead curriculum review processes that address both vertical coherence within subjects and horizontal coherence across subjects.
Analyse Cambridge syllabus updates and internal assessment data to evaluate curriculum quality across multiple departments.
Build shared leadership responsibility for curriculum quality by engaging department heads in structured review and planning processes.
selfAssessment: I can design and lead a cross-departmental curriculum review process that addresses both vertical and horizontal coherence.
I can use Cambridge syllabus updates, assessment data, and external benchmarks to evaluate the quality of the curriculum across a programme or phase.
I can facilitate collaborative sessions where department heads critically evaluate their curriculum maps and make evidence-based improvements.
I can produce a curriculum quality report for senior leadership that identifies strengths and priorities across multiple departments.
I can coach department heads to lead curriculum review in their own areas using structured frameworks and evidence.

activity.task: Coordinate a cross-departmental curriculum review across at least three subjects in one Cambridge phase. Facilitate a joint planning session and produce a curriculum quality report.
activity.output: A curriculum quality report (3–4 pages) covering: scope and sequence analysis, key strengths across departments, priority areas for development, and recommended actions.
activity.evidence: Completed curriculum quality report; records of cross-departmental meetings; updated curriculum maps or planning documents from participating departments.

lead.reading: At the Lead/Mentor level, you shape the school's approach to curriculum review and vertical coherence as a whole-school system. You design the policies, frameworks, and review cycles that all departments use. You ensure that curriculum design principles — such as progression, challenge, and coherence — are embedded in the school's planning culture. You mentor other leaders to take ownership of curriculum quality in their own areas. You also ensure that the school's curriculum meets Cambridge accreditation requirements and complies with Indonesian national curriculum obligations under Permendikbud. PSEL Standard 4 calls on lead leaders to build a coherent, whole-school curriculum that is intellectually challenging and equitable for all students. Cambridge School Leader Standard 2 requires the most effective leaders to create systems that sustain curriculum quality across the whole school. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 places responsibility for whole-school curriculum management with the principal. At this level, curriculum quality is a school-wide culture you have built — not just a process you manage.
keyTakeaways: Design the school's whole-school curriculum review framework, including policies, cycles, and quality standards used by all departments.
Ensure curriculum design meets Cambridge accreditation standards and Indonesian national curriculum requirements simultaneously.
Mentor other leaders to develop strong curriculum leadership capacity in their own departments.
selfAssessment: I can design a whole-school curriculum review policy and annual review cycle that all departments follow.
I can ensure that the school's curriculum meets the requirements of both Cambridge accreditation and Permendikbud national curriculum regulations.
I can mentor two or more department heads to develop their curriculum leadership skills using structured coaching and evidence-based conversations.
I can present the school's curriculum design and review process to Cambridge representatives or external evaluators.
I can evaluate the long-term quality of the school's curriculum by analysing trends in student outcomes and stakeholder feedback across multiple years.

activity.task: Design or significantly revise the school's whole-school curriculum review policy and framework. Pilot the framework with two department heads and present the findings to senior leadership.
activity.output: A whole-school curriculum review framework document (4–6 pages) including: curriculum design principles, review cycle, quality standards, and guidance for department heads. A short presentation summary (1 page) for senior leadership.
activity.evidence: Final framework document; mentoring logs from work with department heads; evidence of senior leadership presentation; feedback from pilot departments.

practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, you conduct lesson observations and provide structured instructional feedback to teachers in your team or department. Lesson observation is a professional development tool — it helps teachers improve their practice when feedback is specific, honest, and constructive. At this level, you use a clear observation framework to record what you see during lessons, and you hold structured pre- and post-observation conversations with teachers. Your feedback focuses on the quality of student learning — not just teaching activity — and is grounded in evidence from the lesson. PSEL Standard 6 calls on leaders to develop the professional capacity of staff through purposeful observation and feedback. Cambridge School Leader Standard 4 requires leaders to support teacher development through professional dialogue. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies teacher supervision and development as a key managerial competency for school leaders. At this stage, your observations contribute directly to teacher growth and to the quality of learning in your department.
keyTakeaways: Use a clear observation framework to record what you see in lessons, focusing on the quality of student learning.
Hold structured pre- and post-observation conversations that make feedback specific, evidence-based, and actionable.
Approach observation as a professional development tool — not an inspection or evaluation exercise.
selfAssessment: I can conduct a full lesson observation using a structured framework and record specific evidence about the quality of student learning.
I can hold a pre-observation conversation with a teacher to clarify the lesson's focus and identify what feedback will be most useful.
I can deliver post-observation feedback that is specific, evidence-based, and focused on improving student learning outcomes.
I can identify two or three concrete actions a teacher can take to improve their practice, based on observation evidence.
I can maintain professional relationships with teachers during and after observations, ensuring the process feels supportive rather than evaluative.

activity.task: Conduct lesson observations for at least three teachers in your department over one term. Hold structured pre- and post-observation conversations, and produce a department observation summary.
activity.output: Individual observation records for each teacher (using a standard framework) and a one-page department summary identifying common strengths and shared development priorities.
activity.evidence: Completed observation records; notes from pre- and post-observation conversations; department observation summary shared with school leadership.

advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, you lead the lesson observation process across a programme or phase. You develop or adapt the observation framework your school uses. You train and support other leaders to conduct high-quality observations and deliver effective instructional feedback. You use observation data from multiple classrooms to identify whole-school patterns in teaching and learning — such as consistent strengths, common areas for development, or specific approaches that need attention. You also connect observation findings to the school's professional development priorities. PSEL Standard 6 requires leaders to create conditions for continuous teacher learning, using observation and feedback as central tools. Cambridge School Leader Standard 4 calls on leaders to ensure that teacher appraisal and development processes are fair, rigorous, and improvement-focused. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 includes teacher supervision as an ongoing responsibility of school leadership. At this level, lesson observation is a strategic tool you use to drive school-wide instructional improvement.
keyTakeaways: Lead and quality-assure the observation process across multiple departments, ensuring consistency and fairness.
Use aggregated observation data to identify patterns in teaching quality and connect findings to professional development priorities.
Train and support other leaders to conduct high-quality observations and deliver effective, growth-focused feedback.
selfAssessment: I can develop or adapt a lesson observation framework that is suitable for use across multiple departments and year groups.
I can train other leaders to conduct observations using the school's framework and provide feedback on the quality of their observation practice.
I can analyse observation data from multiple teachers and departments to identify patterns in teaching quality across a programme or phase.
I can use observation findings to design or adjust the school's professional development priorities for the following term.
I can produce a structured teaching quality report for senior leadership, using aggregated observation evidence as the primary data source.

activity.task: Lead a phase-level lesson observation programme for one term. Analyse aggregated data from observations conducted across at least three departments and produce a teaching quality report.
activity.output: A teaching quality report (2–3 pages) with: summary of observation data, key patterns identified, teaching strengths and development priorities, and recommendations for professional development action.
activity.evidence: Completed observation records from multiple departments; teaching quality report; evidence of professional development actions taken in response to findings.

lead.reading: At the Lead/Mentor level, you design the school's whole observation and instructional feedback system. You create the policies, frameworks, and training that ensure all leaders can observe lessons and give effective feedback. You build a school culture where observation is understood and accepted as a professional development tool — not a performance management mechanism. You mentor other leaders to continuously improve their observation and feedback skills. You use observation data strategically to evaluate teaching quality across the whole school and to inform whole-school professional development planning. PSEL Standard 6 calls on lead leaders to build the professional capacity of the whole organisation through systems that support continuous teacher growth. Cambridge School Leader Standard 4 requires the most effective leaders to embed observation and feedback into the school's development culture. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 places overall responsibility for teacher supervision with the school principal. At this level, high-quality teaching is a system outcome — not just an individual achievement.
keyTakeaways: Design the school's whole observation and feedback system, including the policy, frameworks, training programme, and quality-assurance process.
Build a school culture where observation is valued as professional development by both teachers and leaders.
Use whole-school observation data strategically to evaluate teaching quality and inform professional development planning at system level.
selfAssessment: I can design and implement a whole-school lesson observation policy that includes clear purposes, processes, and quality-assurance mechanisms.
I can train and mentor school leaders to conduct high-quality observations and deliver growth-focused instructional feedback consistently.
I can use whole-school observation data to evaluate trends in teaching quality over time and report findings to the school board.
I can connect observation findings to long-term professional development strategy, ensuring that CPD addresses the actual needs identified through evidence.
I can evaluate the effectiveness of the school's observation and feedback system annually and make evidence-based improvements.

activity.task: Review the school's current lesson observation policy and framework. Identify gaps, revise the system, train two leaders to use the revised framework, and present the updated system to senior leadership.
activity.output: A revised whole-school observation policy and framework document (3–5 pages) including: purpose statement, observation process, feedback guidelines, quality-assurance process, and a training guide for leaders.
activity.evidence: Final policy and framework document; training records for two leaders; evidence of quality-assurance review; record of presentation to senior leadership.

awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, you are beginning to understand how to read and interpret Cambridge examination results data. Cambridge provides schools with a range of results reports — including grade distributions, subject performance summaries, and comparison data against global and regional benchmarks. At this level, you learn how to read these reports and understand what the numbers mean. You begin to ask basic questions about your department's results: Did students perform better or worse than expected? Which assessment components were most challenging? How does our performance compare to the global average? PSEL Standard 10 states that effective leaders use data and evidence to evaluate the quality of learning and teaching. Cambridge School Leader Standard 3 calls on leaders to use examination data as part of the school's self-evaluation process. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 includes evaluation and data use as core managerial competencies for school principals. At this stage, your goal is to become comfortable reading results data and identifying simple patterns that can guide your department's next steps.
keyTakeaways: Learn how to read Cambridge results reports, including grade distributions and benchmark comparisons.
Begin asking analytical questions about your department's results — strengths, weaknesses, and unexpected patterns.
Connect results data to teaching and curriculum decisions in your department.
selfAssessment: I can read a Cambridge subject results report and identify the grade distribution for my department's students.
I can compare my department's results to the Cambridge global average and describe whether performance was above, below, or in line with expectations.
I can identify at least two specific questions to investigate further based on patterns in my department's results.
I can explain what PSEL Standard 10 says about using data to evaluate educational quality.
I can describe one curriculum or teaching decision that could be informed by my department's most recent Cambridge results.

activity.task: Analyse the most recent Cambridge results report for one subject in your department. Write a short data summary identifying key patterns and one area for follow-up investigation.
activity.output: A one-page results data summary with: grade distribution overview, comparison to global benchmark, two or three key patterns identified, and one recommended follow-up question.
activity.evidence: Completed data summary document; Cambridge results report used as the data source.

practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, you use Cambridge results data and benchmarking information consistently to evaluate teaching and learning in your department. You go beyond reading results — you investigate the reasons behind the numbers. You compare performance across year groups, question paper components, and student sub-groups such as gender or language background. You also look for trends across multiple examination sessions, not just the most recent results. You use your analysis to make specific and evidence-based decisions about curriculum, teaching, and intervention priorities. PSEL Standard 10 calls on leaders to use multiple data sources to drive continuous improvement. Cambridge School Leader Standard 3 requires leaders to use examination results as part of a structured self-evaluation process. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies data-driven evaluation as a key managerial competency for principals. At this stage, your results analysis leads directly to departmental action — it is not just a reporting exercise.
keyTakeaways: Investigate results data in depth — looking at component-level performance, trends over time, and variation across student groups.
Use Cambridge benchmarking data to place your department's performance in a wider context.
Translate analysis findings into specific, evidence-based decisions about curriculum, teaching, and student support.
selfAssessment: I can analyse Cambridge results across multiple components and identify where students performed most and least strongly.
I can examine results trends across two or more examination sessions to identify patterns of improvement or decline in my department.
I can disaggregate results by student sub-groups — such as gender, language background, or prior attainment — and identify significant differences in performance.
I can use Cambridge benchmarking data to evaluate my department's performance in a global context and explain the findings to school leadership.
I can produce a department results analysis report with specific recommendations for curriculum, teaching, or intervention priorities.

activity.task: Produce a full results analysis report for your department covering the last two Cambridge examination sessions. Include component-level data, trend analysis, benchmark comparison, and targeted recommendations.
activity.output: A department results analysis report (2–3 pages) with: grade distribution summary, component analysis, trend comparison across two sessions, benchmark data, and three or more specific recommendations.
activity.evidence: Completed results analysis report; Cambridge results data used; record of discussion with department teachers about the findings and next steps.

advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, you lead Cambridge results analysis and benchmarking across a programme or phase. You synthesise data from multiple subjects and year groups to identify whole-school patterns in academic performance. You evaluate whether the school's curriculum, teaching, and intervention strategies are producing the results that students deserve. You present your analysis to senior leaders and external stakeholders in a clear, structured, and evidence-based way. You also help other leaders develop their capacity to analyse and use results data effectively. PSEL Standard 10 requires leaders to use data systems to continuously evaluate and improve teaching and learning at scale. Cambridge School Leader Standard 3 calls on leaders to use evaluation evidence to improve quality across the whole school. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 includes whole-school performance evaluation as a principal-level responsibility. At this level, your analysis shapes school-wide decisions about curriculum, professional development, and student support.
keyTakeaways: Synthesise Cambridge results data across multiple subjects and year groups to identify school-wide performance patterns.
Evaluate whether school strategies — in curriculum, teaching, and intervention — are producing measurable improvements in results.
Present analysis findings clearly to senior leadership and use them to drive decisions about whole-school priorities.
selfAssessment: I can synthesise Cambridge results data across multiple subjects and produce a whole-programme performance summary with clear patterns and priorities.
I can evaluate whether the school's academic interventions are producing measurable improvements in Cambridge results over time.
I can use Cambridge benchmarking data and external performance comparators to make evidence-based judgements about the school's academic standards.
I can present a results analysis report to senior leadership or the school board with clear findings, recommendations, and proposed actions.
I can coach department heads to improve the quality of their own results analysis processes and reporting.

activity.task: Produce a whole-programme results analysis report covering at least three Cambridge subjects across one full examination cycle. Present the findings to senior leadership with recommendations.
activity.output: A whole-programme results analysis report (4–5 pages) covering: subject-by-subject summaries, cross-subject patterns, benchmark comparisons, trend analysis, and strategic recommendations for the following academic year.
activity.evidence: Completed whole-programme report; Cambridge results data used; presentation record or slides from senior leadership meeting; decisions made in response to the report.

lead.reading: At the Lead/Mentor level, you design the school's whole data analysis system for Cambridge results and academic benchmarking. You create the processes, tools, and routines that allow all leaders to analyse results data accurately and use it to drive improvement. You ensure that results analysis is connected to the school's improvement planning, professional development, and accountability processes. You also represent the school's academic performance to external stakeholders — including Cambridge, parent communities, and government bodies — in a clear and credible way. PSEL Standard 10 calls on lead leaders to establish data systems that build a culture of evidence-based decision-making across the whole school. Cambridge School Leader Standard 3 requires the most effective leaders to embed self-evaluation data into all aspects of school leadership and governance. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 places responsibility for whole-school data management and reporting with the principal. At this level, data use is a whole-school culture you have built over time.
keyTakeaways: Design the school's whole data analysis system for Cambridge results, including the processes, tools, and reporting structures used by all leaders.
Connect results analysis explicitly to school improvement planning, professional development priorities, and leadership accountability.
Represent the school's academic performance credibly to Cambridge, parent communities, and regulatory bodies.
selfAssessment: I can design a whole-school data analysis system that provides all leaders with clear, accessible Cambridge results data and standard reporting formats.
I can connect Cambridge results analysis to the school improvement plan, ensuring that data findings drive specific improvement priorities each year.
I can present the school's academic performance — including trends, benchmarks, and strategic responses — to the school board, Cambridge representatives, or government bodies.
I can mentor two or more leaders to strengthen their capacity to analyse and use results data effectively in their own areas.
I can evaluate the quality of the school's data analysis culture over time and make systemic improvements when the evidence shows they are needed.

activity.task: Design or significantly revise the school's annual Cambridge results analysis cycle, including templates, reporting timelines, and leadership responsibilities. Train two leaders to use the system and present the design to senior leadership.
activity.output: A whole-school results analysis system document (4–6 pages) including: analysis cycle calendar, standard reporting templates, data interpretation guidance, and a summary of leadership responsibilities. Include mentoring logs from work with two leaders.
activity.evidence: Final system document; training records; mentoring logs; evidence of system use in the most recent examination cycle; record of senior leadership presentation.

advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, you lead the development and quality assurance of assessment systems across a programme or phase. Assessment systems include internal assessment policies, formative and summative assessment practices, and Cambridge coursework or portfolio requirements. At this level, you design and implement moderation processes that ensure assessments are marked consistently and fairly across classes and teachers. You facilitate moderation meetings where teachers review marked work together and reach agreement about standards. You also evaluate whether current assessment practices are providing students and teachers with useful information about learning progress. PSEL Standard 4 calls on leaders to ensure that assessment systems are rigorous, equitable, and aligned to curriculum goals. Cambridge School Leader Standard 2 requires leaders to maintain high standards of assessment quality across the school. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies student assessment and evaluation as a core managerial responsibility for principals. At this level, your leadership ensures that all students are assessed fairly and that assessment data is useful and reliable.
keyTakeaways: Design and lead moderation processes that ensure assessment marking is consistent and fair across classes and teachers.
Evaluate whether assessment practices are providing useful information for teachers and students about learning progress.
Connect assessment system quality to Cambridge coursework requirements and school-wide assessment policy.
selfAssessment: I can design and lead a moderation process for at least one Cambridge assessment component, ensuring marking consistency across multiple teachers.
I can facilitate a moderation meeting where teachers review student work together, reach agreement on standards, and document the outcomes.
I can evaluate the quality of formative and summative assessment practices across a programme or phase, using data and teacher feedback as evidence.
I can identify weaknesses in current assessment systems — such as inconsistent marking or poor feedback quality — and design specific improvement actions.
I can produce an assessment quality review report for senior leadership that summarises findings and proposes systemic improvements.

activity.task: Design and lead a full moderation cycle for one Cambridge assessment component across at least three classes or teachers. Facilitate the moderation meeting and produce a moderation report.
activity.output: A moderation report (2–3 pages) covering: moderation process used, key decisions made, areas of agreement and disagreement, and recommendations for improving assessment consistency.
activity.evidence: Completed moderation report; moderation meeting records; sample annotated student work used in the process; record of actions taken in response to findings.

lead.reading: At the Lead/Mentor level, you design and steward the school's whole assessment system. You create the policies, structures, and quality-assurance processes that govern how all students are assessed across the school. You ensure that the assessment system is aligned to Cambridge requirements, supports student learning, and provides fair and reliable data for school decision-making. You mentor other leaders to develop their assessment leadership skills, including moderation facilitation, data interpretation, and feedback quality. You also represent the school's assessment practices to Cambridge, accreditation bodies, and government regulators. PSEL Standard 4 requires lead leaders to ensure that assessment systems are coherent, rigorous, and equitable across the whole school. Cambridge School Leader Standard 2 calls on the most effective leaders to embed assessment quality as a school-wide commitment. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 places ultimate responsibility for student assessment governance with the school principal. At this level, assessment quality is a whole-school system outcome — not just the responsibility of individual teachers.
keyTakeaways: Design the school's whole assessment policy and quality-assurance system, covering internal assessments, moderation, and Cambridge requirements.
Mentor other leaders to develop assessment leadership skills, including moderation facilitation and data interpretation.
Represent the school's assessment practices credibly to Cambridge and external evaluation bodies.
selfAssessment: I can design a whole-school assessment policy that defines assessment purposes, types, marking standards, moderation processes, and reporting expectations.
I can create a school-wide moderation calendar and framework that all departments use to ensure assessment consistency across the year.
I can mentor two or more leaders to develop their moderation leadership skills using structured coaching and evidence-based review conversations.
I can present the school's assessment system to Cambridge representatives or external evaluators, demonstrating alignment to Cambridge requirements and best practice.
I can evaluate the effectiveness of the school's assessment system annually, using data from moderation records, student outcomes, and stakeholder feedback.

activity.task: Design or significantly revise the school's whole-school assessment policy and moderation framework. Pilot the framework with two department heads and present the system to senior leadership.
activity.output: A whole-school assessment policy and moderation framework document (5–7 pages) including: assessment philosophy, types and purposes, marking standards, moderation process, reporting expectations, and a guide for department heads.
activity.evidence: Final policy and framework document; pilot feedback from two department heads; mentoring logs; record of senior leadership presentation; evidence of adoption in at least one department's practice.

awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, you are beginning to understand what teacher appraisal means and how development planning supports teacher growth. Teacher appraisal is a structured process that helps schools identify teacher strengths, set development goals, and support professional improvement. At this level, you learn how your school's appraisal system works — including the cycle, the criteria, and the documentation used. You begin to understand how development plans connect individual teacher goals to the school's improvement priorities. You also start to see the difference between appraisal as an accountability tool and appraisal as a genuine development process. PSEL Standard 6 emphasises that effective leaders develop the professional capacity of all staff through structured appraisal. Cambridge School Leader Standard 4 states that leaders must support staff development through purposeful planning and professional dialogue. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies teacher supervision and performance management as core managerial competencies for school principals. At this stage, your focus is on understanding the appraisal system clearly before you begin to lead it.
keyTakeaways: Understand how your school's teacher appraisal cycle works, including its criteria, documentation, and timelines.
Recognise the difference between appraisal as an accountability tool and appraisal as a professional development process.
Begin connecting teacher development goals to school improvement priorities.
selfAssessment: I can explain the structure and purpose of my school's teacher appraisal cycle.
I can identify the appraisal criteria used in my school and explain what each criterion means in practice.
I can describe how a teacher development plan connects individual goals to school-wide improvement priorities.
I can explain what PSEL Standard 6 says about developing professional capacity through structured appraisal.
I can give an example of how the appraisal process in my school could be made more development-focused.

activity.task: Review your school's teacher appraisal documentation — including forms, criteria, and development plan templates — and write a short analytical summary of how the system is designed and how it could better support teacher growth.
activity.output: A one-page written summary with three sections: how the appraisal cycle is structured, how development planning is currently used, and one recommendation to make the process more development-focused.
activity.evidence: Completed summary document; list of appraisal documents reviewed; record of a brief discussion with a mentor or line manager about the findings.

practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, you conduct teacher appraisals and support development planning within your team or department. You hold structured appraisal conversations with teachers that are honest, specific, and growth-focused. You help teachers set development goals that are relevant to their practice and connected to departmental and school priorities. You monitor progress towards development goals through regular check-in conversations — not just formal annual reviews. You also use appraisal findings to identify shared development needs across your team. PSEL Standard 6 calls on leaders to use appraisal as a tool for building teacher capacity, not just measuring performance. Cambridge School Leader Standard 4 requires leaders to conduct fair and purposeful appraisal that supports professional growth. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 includes teacher performance management as a key managerial responsibility of school leaders. At this stage, appraisal is a regular, structured leadership practice that produces real development outcomes for teachers in your team.
keyTakeaways: Conduct appraisal conversations that are honest, specific, and focused on professional growth rather than performance judgement alone.
Help teachers set development goals that connect their individual practice to departmental and school-wide priorities.
Monitor progress towards development goals through regular check-in conversations throughout the year.
selfAssessment: I can conduct a structured appraisal conversation with a teacher that includes honest feedback, goal-setting, and a development plan.
I can help a teacher identify two or three specific, measurable development goals that connect to school priorities.
I can hold regular mid-cycle check-in conversations with teachers to review progress towards their development goals.
I can identify shared development needs across my department based on appraisal evidence and plan a team-level response.
I can complete appraisal documentation accurately and use it as a working record of teacher development — not just a compliance form.

activity.task: Conduct appraisals for at least three teachers in your department during one appraisal cycle. Produce a department development summary based on the findings.
activity.output: Individual teacher development plans for each appraisal conducted and a one-page department development summary identifying shared development priorities and proposed team-level actions.
activity.duration: One full appraisal cycle (term or semester)
activity.evidence: Completed teacher development plans; department development summary; record of mid-cycle check-in conversations.

advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, you lead the appraisal system across a programme or phase. You work with multiple department heads to ensure that appraisals are conducted consistently, fairly, and with genuine development intent. You quality-assure appraisal conversations and development plans — checking that goals are specific and achievable, and that feedback is constructive. You analyse appraisal data across your phase to identify patterns in teacher development needs and use these to inform professional development priorities. PSEL Standard 6 requires leaders to build professional capacity at scale, using appraisal as a strategic lever for school-wide development. Cambridge School Leader Standard 4 calls on leaders to ensure that appraisal processes are rigorous, equitable, and development-focused across the school. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 places responsibility for teacher performance management at the level of the school principal. At this level, your leadership ensures that all teachers across your phase benefit from high-quality appraisal and development planning.
keyTakeaways: Quality-assure appraisal processes across multiple departments to ensure consistency, fairness, and genuine development intent.
Analyse appraisal data across a programme or phase to identify shared development patterns and inform professional development priorities.
Support department heads to conduct high-quality appraisals through coaching, shared frameworks, and structured feedback.
selfAssessment: I can review a sample of teacher development plans from multiple departments and provide constructive feedback on their quality.
I can facilitate a calibration session where department heads align the standards they use in appraisal conversations.
I can analyse appraisal data from across a programme or phase to identify common development priorities and report findings to senior leadership.
I can coach a department head to improve the quality of their appraisal conversations using a structured observation and feedback approach.
I can produce a phase-level development needs analysis report that connects appraisal findings to professional development planning for the following year.

activity.task: Lead a quality-assurance review of the appraisal process across at least three departments. Facilitate a calibration session and produce a development needs analysis report.
activity.output: A development needs analysis report (2–3 pages) covering: appraisal quality review findings, common development priorities identified, and recommended professional development actions for the following year.
activity.evidence: Completed development needs analysis report; record of calibration meeting; feedback provided to department heads on appraisal quality; link to professional development planning decisions.

lead.reading: At the Lead/Mentor level, you design and steward the school's whole teacher appraisal and development system. You create the policy, frameworks, criteria, and processes that all leaders use. You ensure the system is fair, evidence-based, and genuinely development-focused — not just a compliance mechanism. You mentor other leaders to improve their appraisal skills and you evaluate the long-term impact of the system on teacher growth and student outcomes. You also ensure that the appraisal system meets Indonesian regulatory requirements and aligns with Cambridge professional development expectations. PSEL Standard 6 calls on lead leaders to build coherent, whole-school systems for professional capacity development. Cambridge School Leader Standard 4 requires the most effective leaders to embed appraisal into the school's professional culture as a driver of continuous improvement. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 places ultimate accountability for teacher performance management with the school principal. At this level, the appraisal system is a strategic asset you have built and continuously improve.
keyTakeaways: Design the school's whole appraisal policy and framework, ensuring it is development-focused, equitable, and compliant with Indonesian regulatory requirements.
Mentor other leaders to develop their appraisal skills and monitor the impact of the system on teacher growth over time.
Evaluate the long-term effectiveness of the appraisal system using evidence from teacher development outcomes and student results.
selfAssessment: I can design a whole-school teacher appraisal policy that includes clear criteria, processes, documentation standards, and professional development links.
I can ensure that the appraisal system complies with Permendikbud requirements while remaining development-focused and aligned to Cambridge professional standards.
I can mentor two or more school leaders to develop their appraisal skills using structured coaching and evidence-based feedback.
I can evaluate the long-term impact of the appraisal system on teacher development and student outcomes using trend data across multiple years.
I can present the school's appraisal and development system to external evaluators or Cambridge representatives, demonstrating its quality and impact.

activity.task: Review and revise the school's teacher appraisal policy and framework. Conduct a long-term impact review using data from two or more years and mentor two leaders in their appraisal practice.
activity.output: A revised whole-school appraisal policy and framework document (4–6 pages) and a one-page impact review summary showing evidence of the system's effect on teacher development. Include mentoring logs from two structured coaching sessions.
activity.evidence: Final policy and framework document; impact review summary with supporting data; mentoring logs; record of presentation to senior leadership or board.

practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, you plan and coordinate professional development activities for your team or department. CPD — continuing professional development — includes workshops, coaching, collaborative planning, and formal qualifications. At this level, you identify the development needs of teachers in your team and design a CPD plan that addresses those needs. You are also aware of the Cambridge Professional Development Qualifications — including the PDQ Certificate and Diploma — and you can explain these pathways to teachers who may benefit from them. You ensure that CPD activities are relevant, practical, and connected to the school's improvement priorities. PSEL Standard 6 calls on leaders to build teacher capacity through purposeful and targeted professional development. Cambridge School Leader Standard 5 requires leaders to support teachers' continuous professional growth, including through formal Cambridge pathways. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies teacher professional development as a core managerial responsibility of school leaders. At this stage, CPD is a planned and structured part of your leadership — not a collection of unrelated events.
keyTakeaways: Identify the professional development needs of teachers in your team through appraisal data, observation findings, and results analysis.
Design a CPD plan for your department that connects development activities directly to school improvement priorities.
Know the Cambridge PDQ Certificate and Diploma pathways and be able to explain them to teachers who may benefit.
selfAssessment: I can identify the professional development needs of teachers in my department using appraisal evidence, observation data, and student results.
I can design a one-term CPD plan for my department that includes a range of development activities aligned to identified needs.
I can explain the Cambridge PDQ Certificate and Diploma pathways to a teacher and describe how they could support professional growth.
I can evaluate the effectiveness of CPD activities in my department by gathering teacher feedback and looking for changes in practice.
I can connect my department's CPD plan to the school's broader professional development strategy and improvement priorities.

activity.task: Design a one-term CPD plan for your department, based on appraisal and observation evidence. Include at least one activity linked to the Cambridge PDQ pathways.
