activity.output: A written department CPD plan (1–2 pages) with: development needs identified, planned activities, timelines, responsible persons, and an evaluation method for each activity.
activity.evidence: Completed CPD plan; evidence of needs identification such as appraisal data or observation summary; brief evaluation summary from teachers at end of term.

advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, you lead CPD strategy across a programme or phase and support the school's engagement with Cambridge PDQ pathways. You analyse professional development needs across multiple departments and design a phase-level CPD strategy that is evidence-based and connected to school improvement priorities. You identify which teachers would benefit from Cambridge PDQ programmes and support them through the process — including helping them understand requirements, find mentors, and manage their workload. You evaluate the effectiveness of CPD activities and adjust the strategy based on evidence. PSEL Standard 6 requires leaders to build professional capacity across the whole school through strategic and coherent professional development. Cambridge School Leader Standard 5 calls on leaders to build a culture where professional growth — including through Cambridge PDQ — is expected, supported, and celebrated. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 includes teacher professional development oversight as a principal-level responsibility. At this level, CPD is a strategic leadership function you manage with evidence and clear purpose.
keyTakeaways: Design a phase-level CPD strategy based on evidence from appraisal, observation, and results data across multiple departments.
Actively support teachers in your phase to engage with the Cambridge PDQ Certificate and Diploma programmes.
Evaluate the effectiveness of CPD activities and adjust the strategy based on evidence of impact on teaching practice.
selfAssessment: I can analyse professional development needs across multiple departments and produce a phase-level CPD strategy aligned to school improvement priorities.
I can identify which teachers across my phase would benefit from the Cambridge PDQ Certificate or Diploma and support them to begin the process.
I can evaluate the effectiveness of CPD activities across my phase using evidence such as teacher feedback, observation data, and changes in student outcomes.
I can facilitate a professional development session for a group of teachers across departments using a structured facilitation approach.
I can produce a CPD strategy report for senior leadership that summarises needs identified, planned activities, Cambridge PDQ participation, and evaluation outcomes.

activity.task: Design a one-year CPD strategy for your programme or phase. Include a section on Cambridge PDQ participation targets and an evaluation plan. Present the strategy to senior leadership.
activity.output: A phase-level CPD strategy document (2–3 pages) with: development needs summary, planned activities calendar, Cambridge PDQ participation plan, and evaluation framework. Presentation notes for the senior leadership meeting.
activity.evidence: Completed CPD strategy document; presentation record; evidence of Cambridge PDQ teacher engagement; end-of-year CPD evaluation summary.

lead.reading: At the Lead/Mentor level, you design the school's whole CPD strategy and lead its engagement with Cambridge PDQ pathways as an institutional commitment. You create the policies, structures, and culture that make professional development a priority for every teacher — not just those who seek it voluntarily. You build relationships with Cambridge and other professional bodies to bring high-quality development opportunities to your school. You mentor other leaders to develop their capacity to plan and evaluate CPD. You also evaluate the long-term impact of the school's CPD investment on teacher quality, retention, and student outcomes. PSEL Standard 6 calls on lead leaders to build sustainable systems for professional capacity development across the whole organisation. Cambridge School Leader Standard 5 requires the most effective leaders to make teacher professional growth a structural feature of the school — not a peripheral activity. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 places responsibility for the professional development of all teaching staff with the school principal. At this level, professional development is a core pillar of how your school operates.
keyTakeaways: Design the school's whole CPD policy and strategy, ensuring professional development is connected to appraisal, improvement planning, and Cambridge PDQ participation.
Build institutional relationships with Cambridge and professional bodies to expand the quality and range of development opportunities available to staff.
Evaluate the long-term impact of the school's CPD investment on teacher quality, staff retention, and student outcomes.
selfAssessment: I can design a whole-school CPD policy and multi-year strategy that connects professional development to appraisal, improvement planning, and Cambridge PDQ.
I can build and maintain partnerships with Cambridge and other professional bodies to expand CPD opportunities for staff across the school.
I can mentor two or more leaders to develop their skills in planning, facilitating, and evaluating professional development in their own areas.
I can evaluate the long-term impact of the school's CPD strategy using evidence from teacher development outcomes, Cambridge PDQ completion rates, and student results.
I can present the school's CPD strategy and impact data to the school board, Cambridge representatives, or government bodies in a credible and evidence-based way.

activity.task: Design or significantly revise the school's whole-school CPD policy and multi-year strategy. Include an analysis of Cambridge PDQ participation to date and a plan to expand engagement. Mentor two leaders in their CPD planning.
activity.output: A whole-school CPD policy and multi-year strategy document (4–6 pages) including: development philosophy, annual planning cycle, Cambridge PDQ pathway framework, evaluation process, and leadership responsibilities. Include mentoring logs from two structured sessions.
activity.evidence: Final CPD policy and strategy document; Cambridge PDQ participation data; mentoring logs; record of board or senior leadership presentation.

advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, you provide structured coaching and mentoring to teachers and emerging leaders in your school. Coaching and mentoring are different from giving advice — they involve asking good questions, listening carefully, and helping people find their own solutions. At this level, you develop and use a consistent coaching approach — such as a structured questioning framework — in your regular professional conversations. You also identify teachers who show leadership potential and create opportunities for them to take on greater responsibility. You support these teachers through regular mentoring conversations and guided practice. PSEL Standard 7 calls on leaders to build a professional community where teachers learn from one another and grow into leadership roles. Cambridge School Leader Standard 5 requires leaders to develop teacher leadership capacity through coaching, mentoring, and structured development opportunities. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies the development of teacher and staff capacity as a core principal responsibility. At this level, you invest in individuals — and in doing so, you build the leadership pipeline your school needs.
keyTakeaways: Use a structured coaching approach — not advice-giving — in your regular professional conversations with teachers and emerging leaders.
Identify teachers with leadership potential and create opportunities for them to develop through guided practice and responsibility.
Hold regular mentoring conversations with emerging leaders using a consistent structure that supports reflection and growth.
selfAssessment: I can use a structured coaching framework — such as the GROW model or a similar approach — in a professional conversation with a teacher or emerging leader.
I can identify at least two teachers in my phase or programme who show leadership potential, and describe a development plan for each.
I can conduct a structured mentoring conversation with an emerging leader that includes goal-setting, reflection, and agreed next steps.
I can distinguish clearly between coaching, mentoring, and line management, and explain when each approach is most appropriate.
I can document coaching and mentoring conversations in a way that supports accountability and tracks the growth of the person I am supporting.

activity.task: Provide structured coaching or mentoring to two teachers or emerging leaders over one term. Use a consistent framework and document each session.
activity.output: A coaching or mentoring log covering at least four sessions per person — eight sessions in total — each with: date, focus, key discussion points, agreed next steps, and progress notes.
activity.evidence: Completed mentoring or coaching logs; evidence of the framework used; reflective notes on your own coaching practice and areas for development.

lead.reading: At the Lead/Mentor level, you build the school's coaching and mentoring culture and develop teacher leadership as a whole-school system. You design the frameworks, training, and processes that allow coaching and mentoring to happen consistently across the school — not just in individual relationships. You train other leaders to use coaching approaches in their everyday conversations. You create formal teacher leadership pathways — such as subject leader roles, curriculum lead positions, or research lead responsibilities — that allow capable teachers to grow into broader leadership. You also evaluate whether the school's coaching and mentoring culture is producing measurable improvements in teacher retention, professional satisfaction, and leadership capacity. PSEL Standard 7 requires lead leaders to build professional community and teacher leadership as structural features of the school. Cambridge School Leader Standard 5 calls on the most effective leaders to develop a school-wide coaching culture that sustains professional growth at all levels. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 places responsibility for staff development and leadership capacity building with the school principal. At this level, coaching and mentoring are not activities you do — they are a culture you have created.
keyTakeaways: Design the school's coaching and mentoring framework, training programme, and quality-assurance process for use by all leaders.
Create formal teacher leadership pathways that give capable teachers structured opportunities to grow into broader school leadership.
Evaluate the impact of the school's coaching culture on teacher growth, retention, and leadership pipeline development.
selfAssessment: I can design a whole-school coaching and mentoring framework that defines the purpose, approach, structure, and quality-assurance process.
I can train other school leaders to use coaching approaches in their professional conversations and provide feedback on the quality of their coaching practice.
I can design and implement formal teacher leadership pathways that create structured development opportunities for capable teachers.
I can evaluate the effectiveness of the school's coaching and mentoring culture using evidence from teacher surveys, retention data, and leadership pipeline analysis.
I can present the school's coaching and mentoring strategy and its impact to the school board, Cambridge representatives, or external evaluators.

activity.task: Design a whole-school coaching and mentoring framework and teacher leadership pathway plan. Pilot the framework with two leaders and present the design to senior leadership.
activity.output: A whole-school coaching and mentoring framework document (3–5 pages) including: coaching philosophy, process guidelines, session structure, documentation requirements, and quality-assurance approach. A teacher leadership pathway plan (1–2 pages) showing progression routes and role responsibilities.
activity.evidence: Final framework and pathway plan; training records from work with two leaders; pilot feedback; record of senior leadership presentation.

advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, you lead the development of a professional learning culture across a programme or phase. A professional learning culture is one where teachers reflect on their practice, share ideas, learn from each other, and continuously improve — not just when required, but because they value growth. At this level, you design and facilitate collaborative learning structures — such as professional learning communities, peer observation cycles, or lesson study groups — that make professional learning a regular and valued part of teachers' work. You evaluate whether these structures are producing genuine learning and improvement, and you adjust your approach based on evidence. PSEL Standard 7 calls on leaders to build a professional community characterised by collective learning and shared responsibility for student success. Cambridge School Leader Standard 5 requires leaders to create conditions where teachers learn and grow together. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies the development of a positive school work culture as a core managerial competency for principals. At this level, professional learning is not something you add to the week — it is built into how your phase or programme operates.
keyTakeaways: Design and lead collaborative professional learning structures — such as PLCs, peer observation cycles, or lesson study — that make learning a regular part of teachers' professional lives.
Evaluate whether collaborative learning structures are producing genuine changes in practice, and adjust them when evidence shows they are not working.
Build a culture where teachers share, reflect, and learn together — not just in formal sessions, but in everyday professional conversation.
selfAssessment: I can design and facilitate a professional learning community or collaborative learning structure that meets regularly and has a clear improvement focus.
I can evaluate the effectiveness of collaborative learning activities in my phase or programme using teacher feedback and evidence of changes in practice.
I can identify and address barriers that prevent teachers from engaging fully in professional learning — such as time constraints, low trust, or unclear purpose.
I can create conditions where teacher-led professional learning is valued and encouraged — for example, by giving teachers time, space, and recognition for sharing their practice.
I can present a professional learning culture review to senior leadership, showing evidence of structures in place, teacher engagement, and impact on practice.

activity.task: Design and lead a professional learning community or collaborative learning cycle for your programme or phase over one term. Evaluate its effectiveness at the end of the term.
activity.output: A PLC or collaborative learning cycle plan (1–2 pages) with: focus area, meeting schedule, structure for each session, and evaluation method. An end-of-term evaluation report (1 page) summarising teacher engagement, key learning, and impact on practice.
activity.evidence: Completed PLC or cycle plan; session records or meeting notes; end-of-term evaluation report; teacher feedback collected.

lead.reading: At the Lead/Mentor level, you build the school's professional learning culture as a whole-school system. You create the structures, norms, and values that make continuous professional learning part of how the school works at every level. You ensure that time is protected for professional learning, that leadership learning is as valued as teacher learning, and that the school's professional culture is visible in its everyday practices — not just in formal programmes. You mentor other leaders to build professional learning cultures in their own areas. You also evaluate the school's professional learning culture using evidence from teacher surveys, retention data, and improvements in student outcomes. PSEL Standard 7 calls on lead leaders to build a whole-school professional community where collective learning and shared responsibility are the norm. Cambridge School Leader Standard 5 requires the most effective leaders to make professional growth a defining feature of the school's identity and culture. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 places responsibility for school work culture with the school principal. At this level, the school's reputation as a learning organisation is something you have built — and actively sustain.
keyTakeaways: Build whole-school structures, norms, and protected time that make professional learning visible, valued, and routine for all staff.
Mentor other leaders to develop professional learning cultures in their own departments or phases.
Evaluate the school's professional learning culture using evidence from multiple sources and use the findings to sustain and improve it over time.
selfAssessment: I can design a whole-school professional learning culture strategy that defines what professional learning looks like, how it is structured, and how it is evaluated.
I can create protected time and school structures that allow meaningful collaborative professional learning to happen regularly for all staff.
I can mentor two or more leaders to develop professional learning cultures in their own areas using structured coaching and shared reflection.
I can evaluate the school's professional learning culture using evidence from teacher surveys, retention data, CPD records, and student outcome trends.
I can represent the school as a professional learning community to Cambridge networks, external partners, and accreditation bodies.

activity.task: Design a whole-school professional learning culture strategy and present it to senior leadership. Mentor two leaders in building professional learning cultures in their own areas.
activity.output: A whole-school professional learning culture strategy document (3–5 pages) including: culture principles, structural commitments (time, space, resources), leadership responsibilities, evaluation framework, and a two-year development plan. Include mentoring logs from two structured sessions.
activity.evidence: Final strategy document; mentoring logs; record of senior leadership presentation; evidence of at least one structural change made in response to the strategy.

awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, you are beginning to understand what inclusive education and culturally responsive practice mean in a school leadership context. Inclusive education means that all students — regardless of their learning needs, language background, cultural identity, or socioeconomic status — have equal access to high-quality learning. Culturally responsive practice means that teachers and leaders recognise, respect, and build on the cultural identities and experiences of students and families. At this level, you learn what these principles mean in your school's specific context — including the diversity of your student community and the expectations set by Indonesian education policy. PSEL Standard 3 states that effective leaders act with cultural competence and work to ensure equitable access and opportunity for all students. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 emphasises that leaders must ensure the school's vision is inclusive and serves all learners. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies the promotion of equal educational opportunity as a core managerial competency for school principals. At this stage, your focus is on understanding inclusion and cultural responsiveness before you begin to lead them.
keyTakeaways: Understand what inclusive education means in practice and how it applies in your school's specific cultural and community context.
Recognise the diversity within your student community — including learning needs, language backgrounds, and cultural identities.
Begin identifying how current school practices support or create barriers to inclusion for different groups of students.
selfAssessment: I can explain what inclusive education means in the context of my school's student community.
I can identify the main sources of diversity in my school — including learning needs, language backgrounds, and cultural identities.
I can describe one current school practice that supports inclusion and one that may create barriers for some students.
I can explain what PSEL Standard 3 says about equity and cultural responsiveness in school leadership.
I can give an example of how culturally responsive practice could be applied in my department or year group.

activity.task: Conduct a brief inclusion audit of your department or year group. Review current practices — including teaching approaches, resources, and communication with families — and identify two strengths and two areas for improvement related to inclusive and culturally responsive practice.
activity.output: A one-page written inclusion audit summary with: two current strengths, two areas for development, and one practical action your team can take next term.
activity.evidence: Completed audit summary; list of practices reviewed; record of discussion with at least two colleagues about the findings.

practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, you apply inclusive education principles and culturally responsive practice consistently within your team or department. You review teaching resources, assessment approaches, and communication practices to ensure they are accessible and relevant to all students. You support teachers to differentiate their teaching for students with different learning needs, language backgrounds, or cultural identities. You also work to ensure that family communication is respectful and accessible to all communities in your school. PSEL Standard 3 calls on leaders to ensure that curriculum, instruction, and school practices are equitable and culturally responsive. Cambridge School Leader Standard 2 requires leaders to ensure that all students have access to high-quality learning, regardless of their background or needs. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies inclusive education management as a key managerial responsibility of school leaders. At this stage, inclusion is not a special programme — it is embedded in how your department teaches, assesses, and communicates every day.
keyTakeaways: Review department resources, teaching approaches, and assessments to ensure they are accessible and relevant to all students.
Support teachers to differentiate their practice for students with different learning needs, language backgrounds, or cultural identities.
Ensure family communication from your department is respectful, clear, and accessible to all communities in the school.
selfAssessment: I can review the teaching resources used in my department and identify where changes are needed to make them more inclusive or culturally relevant.
I can support a teacher to differentiate a lesson or assessment task for a student with specific learning needs or language support requirements.
I can ensure that my department's communication with families is clear, respectful, and accessible to families from different language and cultural backgrounds.
I can facilitate a department conversation about inclusive practice, helping teachers identify and address barriers to inclusion in their own classrooms.
I can produce a department inclusion action plan with specific, measurable goals for improving equity in teaching, assessment, and family communication.

activity.task: Lead a department review of inclusive practice, focusing on teaching resources, differentiation strategies, and family communication. Produce a department inclusion action plan.
activity.output: A department inclusion action plan (1–2 pages) with: areas reviewed, key findings, three or more specific improvement actions, responsible persons, and a timeline.
activity.evidence: Completed inclusion action plan; record of department review meeting; evidence of at least one change made to resources or practice as a result.

advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, you lead inclusive education and culturally responsive practice across a programme or phase. You work with multiple department heads to embed inclusion principles into curriculum design, teaching practice, and student support systems. You analyse school data — including attendance, attainment, and wellbeing indicators — to identify which student groups may be underserved, and you design targeted responses. You also build shared leadership responsibility for equity among the leaders in your phase. PSEL Standard 3 requires leaders to ensure that the school acts with cultural competence and creates equitable conditions for all students to succeed. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 calls on leaders to ensure the school's vision actively promotes inclusion and serves all learners equitably. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 includes the promotion of a non-discriminatory and inclusive school environment as a principal-level responsibility. At this level, inclusion is a shared leadership commitment — not the responsibility of individual teachers or support staff alone.
keyTakeaways: Analyse school data — including attendance, attainment, and wellbeing indicators — to identify which student groups may be underserved, and design targeted responses.
Embed inclusion principles into curriculum design, assessment, and student support systems across multiple departments.
Build shared leadership responsibility for equity by engaging department heads in structured review and improvement planning.
selfAssessment: I can analyse attainment, attendance, and wellbeing data across my phase to identify student groups that may be experiencing inequitable outcomes.
I can work with department heads to embed inclusive practice into curriculum planning, teaching approaches, and assessment design.
I can design or review student support systems in my phase to ensure they are responsive to students with learning needs, language support requirements, or cultural challenges.
I can facilitate a cross-departmental discussion about equity that results in specific, shared commitments to improve inclusive practice.
I can produce a phase-level inclusion review report for senior leadership that summarises data findings, priorities, and recommended actions.

activity.task: Lead a phase-level inclusion review using school data. Facilitate a cross-departmental discussion about findings and produce a whole-phase inclusion improvement report.
activity.output: A phase-level inclusion improvement report (3–4 pages) covering: data summary, identified student groups needing more support, current strengths, priority improvement areas, and recommended actions.
activity.evidence: Completed inclusion improvement report; data sources used; record of cross-departmental meeting; evidence of at least one system-level change made in response.

lead.reading: At the Lead/Mentor level, you shape the school's whole approach to inclusive education and culturally responsive practice as a systemic commitment. You design the policies, frameworks, and professional development structures that ensure all students — regardless of background, need, or identity — have equal access to high-quality education. You mentor other leaders to develop their inclusive leadership skills. You also represent the school's inclusion commitments to external stakeholders — including Cambridge, the Indonesian government, and the wider community. PSEL Standard 3 calls on lead leaders to ensure the school is defined by equity, cultural competence, and a commitment to every student's success. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 requires the most effective leaders to build an inclusive vision that is lived throughout the school community. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 places responsibility for inclusive school management with the school principal. At this level, equity is not a goal you are working towards — it is a value embedded in everything your school does.
keyTakeaways: Design the school's whole inclusion policy and framework, ensuring equitable access and culturally responsive practice are embedded in all school systems.
Mentor other leaders to develop inclusive leadership skills and monitor the school's progress on equity over time.
Represent the school's inclusion commitments credibly to Cambridge, government regulators, and the wider community.
selfAssessment: I can design a whole-school inclusive education policy that covers identification of learning needs, support systems, culturally responsive practice, and family engagement.
I can ensure that the school's inclusion practices meet the requirements of Permendikbud regulations and Cambridge's expectations for equitable student access.
I can mentor two or more leaders to develop their inclusive leadership skills using structured coaching and evidence-based review conversations.
I can evaluate the school's progress on inclusion and equity over time using data trends and present findings to the school board.
I can represent the school's inclusion commitments and practices to external bodies — including Cambridge accreditation teams and government inspectors — with confidence and evidence.

activity.task: Design or significantly revise the school's whole-school inclusive education policy and implementation framework. Conduct a long-term equity data review and mentor two leaders on inclusion leadership.
activity.output: A revised whole-school inclusive education policy and framework document (4–6 pages) including: inclusion principles, identification and support processes, culturally responsive practice guidelines, and family engagement approach. A one-page equity data summary showing trends across at least two years. Mentoring logs from two structured sessions.
activity.evidence: Final policy and framework; equity data summary; mentoring logs; record of board or senior leadership presentation.

awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, you are beginning to understand what student wellbeing means in a school leadership context and how monitoring systems work. Student wellbeing covers students' emotional health, sense of safety, social connections, and ability to engage with learning. Good wellbeing does not happen by accident — schools need clear systems to monitor student wellbeing, identify students who need support, and respond effectively. At this level, you learn how your school approaches student wellbeing, what data it collects, and what support services are available. PSEL Standard 5 states that effective leaders work to create a caring, supportive, and safe environment where all students can thrive. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 emphasises that leaders must ensure student wellbeing is a core school priority. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies the promotion of student health and wellbeing as a core managerial competency for school principals. At this stage, your focus is on understanding the school's wellbeing systems and beginning to use them in your area of responsibility.
keyTakeaways: Understand how your school defines and monitors student wellbeing, including what data is collected and how it is used.
Know what support services are available in your school for students who need emotional, social, or academic assistance.
Begin identifying students in your area who may need wellbeing support and understand the referral process.
selfAssessment: I can explain how my school defines student wellbeing and describe the main indicators it uses to monitor student wellbeing.
I can describe the support services available in my school for students with wellbeing needs — including counselling, pastoral care, and external referrals.
I can identify the signs that a student in my area may be experiencing wellbeing challenges and describe the correct referral process.
I can explain what PSEL Standard 5 says about creating a caring and supportive school environment.
I can describe one wellbeing monitoring practice in my school and explain how it helps identify students who need support.

activity.task: Review your school's student wellbeing monitoring system — including the data collected, the referral process, and the support services available. Write a short summary of how the system works and one recommendation to strengthen it in your area.
activity.output: A one-page written summary with three sections: how wellbeing is monitored in your school, how your area currently contributes to wellbeing monitoring, and one recommendation to improve wellbeing support for students in your area.
activity.evidence: Completed summary document; wellbeing system documents reviewed; record of brief discussion with the school counsellor or pastoral lead.

practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, you apply student wellbeing monitoring systems consistently within your team or year group. You use available data — including attendance records, academic performance, and teacher observations — to identify students who may be experiencing wellbeing challenges. You act on this data promptly, following the school's referral and support procedures. You also communicate clearly with parents and guardians when wellbeing concerns arise. You create a team culture where teachers feel responsible for students' emotional health as well as their academic progress. PSEL Standard 5 calls on leaders to create school conditions where all students feel safe, cared for, and ready to learn. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 requires leaders to ensure student wellbeing is monitored and responded to at all levels of the school. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies student welfare management as a key managerial responsibility of school leaders. At this stage, wellbeing monitoring is an active, ongoing practice in your area — not something you respond to only in a crisis.
keyTakeaways: Use attendance, academic, and pastoral data consistently to identify students who may be experiencing wellbeing challenges.
Act on wellbeing concerns promptly and follow the school's referral and support procedures accurately.
Build a team culture where teachers take shared responsibility for monitoring and supporting student wellbeing.
selfAssessment: I can use attendance records, academic data, and teacher pastoral notes to identify students in my area who may be at risk of wellbeing challenges.
I can follow the school's wellbeing referral procedures accurately and document concerns and actions taken in the required format.
I can communicate clearly and sensitively with a parent or guardian about a wellbeing concern involving their child.
I can facilitate a regular pastoral review meeting in my team where teachers share wellbeing concerns and agree on support responses.
I can contribute wellbeing monitoring data and observations to the school's central wellbeing system in a timely and accurate way.

activity.task: Lead a pastoral review cycle for your team or year group over one term, using data to identify students needing support and documenting the actions taken.
activity.output: A term-end pastoral review summary (1–2 pages) covering: number of wellbeing concerns identified, types of support provided, referrals made, and outcomes or next steps for ongoing cases.
activity.evidence: Completed pastoral review summary; pastoral meeting records; referral documentation (anonymised); record of at least two parent or guardian communications.

advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, you lead student wellbeing systems across a programme or phase. You oversee the monitoring of student wellbeing across multiple year groups or departments, ensuring that no student falls through the gaps. You analyse wellbeing data at phase level — including attendance trends, pastoral referrals, and student survey results — to identify patterns and design systemic responses. You work with counsellors, pastoral leads, and external support services to ensure students receive appropriate and timely help. You also report wellbeing findings to senior leadership and recommend improvements to the school's wellbeing systems. PSEL Standard 5 requires leaders to establish a caring, supportive environment and to build the systems that sustain it over time. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 calls on leaders to ensure student wellbeing is a structural priority, not just a reactive response. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 includes whole-school student welfare oversight as a principal-level responsibility. At this level, your leadership ensures the school's wellbeing systems are proactive — identifying and responding to needs before they become crises.
keyTakeaways: Oversee wellbeing monitoring across multiple year groups or departments, ensuring consistent data collection and timely responses.
Analyse phase-level wellbeing data — including attendance, referrals, and student surveys — to identify patterns and design targeted responses.
Work with counsellors, pastoral leads, and external services to ensure all students with wellbeing needs receive appropriate support.
selfAssessment: I can oversee the wellbeing monitoring process across my phase, ensuring that all teams are collecting and using pastoral data consistently.
I can analyse phase-level wellbeing data — including attendance trends and referral records — to identify patterns and report findings to senior leadership.
I can coordinate with school counsellors, pastoral leads, and external support services to design appropriate responses for students with complex wellbeing needs.
I can facilitate a phase-level pastoral review where teachers, counsellors, and leaders share wellbeing data and agree on systemic support responses.
I can produce a phase wellbeing review report for senior leadership that summarises data, identifies priorities, and recommends improvements to the school's wellbeing systems.

activity.task: Lead a phase-level wellbeing data review over one term. Analyse attendance, referral, and survey data, facilitate a pastoral review meeting, and produce a wellbeing review report.
activity.output: A phase-level wellbeing review report (2–3 pages) covering: data summary, patterns identified, current support structures, gaps or weaknesses found, and recommendations for improvement.
activity.evidence: Completed wellbeing review report; data sources used; record of pastoral review meeting; evidence of actions taken in response to the findings.

lead.reading: At the Lead/Mentor level, you design and steward the school's whole student wellbeing system. You create the policies, monitoring structures, referral processes, and staff training that ensure every student's wellbeing is tracked and supported. You build partnerships with external wellbeing and mental health services, and you ensure that the school's response to wellbeing needs is coordinated, evidence-based, and student-centred. You also mentor other leaders to develop their wellbeing leadership skills. You evaluate the long-term effectiveness of the school's wellbeing system and present findings to the school board. PSEL Standard 5 calls on lead leaders to create a whole-school culture of care and to build the systems that protect and promote every student's wellbeing. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 requires the most effective leaders to ensure wellbeing systems are embedded in school governance and reviewed regularly. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 places ultimate responsibility for student welfare with the school principal. At this level, student wellbeing is a whole-school priority backed by strong systems you have designed and maintain.
keyTakeaways: Design the school's whole student wellbeing policy and monitoring system, including referral pathways, support structures, and staff training.
Build partnerships with external wellbeing and mental health services to ensure students with complex needs receive appropriate support.
Evaluate the long-term effectiveness of the school's wellbeing system and present evidence-based findings to the school board.
selfAssessment: I can design a whole-school student wellbeing policy that covers monitoring processes, referral pathways, support structures, and data management.
I can build and maintain partnerships with external wellbeing, mental health, and social services to extend the school's capacity to support students with complex needs.
I can mentor two or more leaders to develop their wellbeing leadership skills using structured coaching and evidence-based feedback.
I can evaluate the long-term effectiveness of the school's wellbeing system using data trends across multiple years and present findings to the board.
I can ensure that the school's wellbeing practices comply with Permendikbud requirements and align with Cambridge's expectations for student welfare.

activity.task: Review and revise the school's student wellbeing policy and monitoring system. Conduct a long-term wellbeing data review and mentor two leaders in wellbeing leadership.
activity.output: A revised whole-school wellbeing policy and system document (4–6 pages) including: wellbeing philosophy, monitoring processes, referral pathways, staff responsibilities, and external partnership framework. A one-page wellbeing data trend summary showing evidence across at least two years. Mentoring logs from two structured sessions.
activity.evidence: Final policy and system document; wellbeing data summary; mentoring logs; record of board or senior leadership presentation.

awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, you are beginning to understand safeguarding and child protection as a leadership responsibility. Safeguarding means taking all reasonable steps to protect children from harm — including physical harm, emotional harm, neglect, and abuse. In a school context, this requires clear policies, trained staff, safe reporting processes, and strong relationships with external child protection bodies. At this level, you learn what your school's safeguarding policy says, who holds key safeguarding responsibilities, and what you are required to do if a concern arises. You also begin to understand the relevant Indonesian legal framework for child protection. PSEL Standard 2 states that effective leaders act ethically and ensure that the school meets its duty of care to all students. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 requires leaders to ensure student safety as a non-negotiable school priority. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies student safety and welfare as core managerial competencies for school principals. At this stage, your focus is on knowing the procedures and understanding your own role in keeping students safe.
keyTakeaways: Know your school's safeguarding policy and understand what you are required to do if you receive or observe a child protection concern.
Understand who holds designated safeguarding responsibilities in your school and how the reporting process works.
Become familiar with the Indonesian legal framework for child protection and how it applies in your school's context.
selfAssessment: I can explain the key principles and requirements of my school's safeguarding and child protection policy.
I can describe the correct procedure to follow if a student discloses a safeguarding concern to me or if I observe a concerning situation.
I can identify the designated safeguarding lead in my school and explain what this person's responsibilities include.
I can explain what PSEL Standard 2 says about the ethical duty of school leaders to protect student safety.
I can describe the relevant Indonesian legal framework for child protection and explain how it applies in my school's context.

activity.task: Review your school's safeguarding policy and related procedures. Write a short briefing note for your team explaining the key safeguarding expectations, reporting procedures, and individual responsibilities.
activity.output: A one-page safeguarding briefing note for a team audience, covering: what safeguarding means, how to recognise a concern, how to report it correctly, and who holds key responsibilities.
activity.evidence: Completed briefing note; safeguarding policy documents reviewed; record of the team briefing session where the note was shared.

practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, you apply safeguarding and child protection responsibilities consistently within your team. You ensure that all staff in your area understand safeguarding procedures and know how to recognise, record, and report concerns. You hold regular briefings on safeguarding expectations and respond promptly and correctly when concerns arise. You also support a culture of vigilance in your team — where safeguarding is seen as everyone's responsibility, not just the designated lead's. PSEL Standard 2 calls on leaders to ensure the school meets its ethical duty of care to all students and to model high standards of professional conduct. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 requires leaders to ensure safeguarding is embedded in daily practice across all areas of the school. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies student welfare management — including protection from harm — as a key managerial responsibility for school leaders. At this stage, safeguarding is not a policy document — it is a live, consistent practice in your area that you actively maintain and monitor.
keyTakeaways: Ensure all staff in your team understand safeguarding procedures and know how to recognise, record, and report concerns correctly.
Respond promptly, accurately, and confidentially when a safeguarding concern is raised in your area.
Build a team culture where safeguarding vigilance is shared and expected — not seen as the sole responsibility of the designated lead.
selfAssessment: I can confirm that all staff in my team have received safeguarding training and understand the school's reporting procedures.
I can respond correctly and confidentially to a safeguarding concern raised by a student, parent, or staff member in my area.
I can complete safeguarding records accurately and submit them to the designated safeguarding lead within the required timeframe.
I can hold a team briefing on safeguarding that covers recognising, recording, and reporting concerns effectively.
I can identify potential safeguarding risks in my area — such as unsafe spaces, poor supervision practices, or concerning staff behaviour — and report these appropriately.

activity.task: Conduct a safeguarding practice review in your team or department. Check that all staff are trained, that procedures are understood, and that records are accurate. Address any gaps found.
activity.output: A one-page safeguarding practice review record with: training status for all staff, any procedural gaps identified, actions taken to address gaps, and confirmation that records are current.
activity.evidence: Completed safeguarding practice review record; evidence of staff training completion; updated safeguarding records; record of actions taken in response to gaps identified.

advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, you lead safeguarding and child protection governance across a programme or phase. You ensure that safeguarding procedures are applied consistently across multiple departments or year groups. You oversee safeguarding training for staff in your phase and conduct regular safeguarding audits to check compliance and identify gaps. You also manage safeguarding concerns that require cross-departmental coordination or escalation to external child protection agencies. You ensure that safeguarding records across your phase are accurate, up to date, and stored securely. PSEL Standard 2 requires leaders to uphold the highest ethical standards and to build school structures that protect every student from harm. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 calls on leaders at this level to lead safeguarding as a governance responsibility — not just a procedural one. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 places responsibility for the safety and protection of all students with the school principal. At this level, safeguarding governance is a structured and evidence-based leadership function you lead with consistency and rigour.
keyTakeaways: Oversee safeguarding training, compliance, and records across multiple departments or year groups in your phase.
Conduct regular safeguarding audits to assess compliance, identify gaps, and drive improvement.
Manage safeguarding concerns that require cross-departmental coordination or escalation to external child protection authorities.
selfAssessment: I can oversee safeguarding training for all staff in my phase and verify that training records are complete and current.
I can conduct a safeguarding audit across at least two departments or year groups and produce a findings report with recommendations.
I can manage a safeguarding concern that involves multiple departments or requires coordination with external child protection agencies.
I can ensure that safeguarding records across my phase are accurate, confidential, securely stored, and comply with school policy and Indonesian legal requirements.
I can produce a phase safeguarding governance report for the designated safeguarding lead or senior leadership, summarising training status, audit findings, and outstanding actions.

activity.task: Conduct a phase-level safeguarding audit covering staff training, recording practices, and procedure compliance. Produce a safeguarding governance report and present findings to the designated safeguarding lead.
activity.output: A safeguarding governance report (2–3 pages) covering: audit scope, training status, compliance findings, gaps identified, and recommended actions with timelines.
activity.evidence: Completed safeguarding governance report; audit records; training verification documents; record of presentation to designated safeguarding lead or senior leadership.

lead.reading: At the Lead/Mentor level, you design and lead the school's whole safeguarding and child protection governance system. You create the policy, training programme, reporting structures, and record-keeping systems that ensure the school meets its full legal and ethical duty of care to all students. You build and maintain relationships with external child protection bodies — including Indonesian government authorities — and ensure the school responds correctly to any external investigations or referrals. You mentor other leaders to develop their safeguarding governance skills. You also ensure the school's safeguarding culture is strong — that every adult in the building understands their responsibility and is equipped to act. PSEL Standard 2 calls on lead leaders to ensure the school is an ethical organisation where student safety is non-negotiable and systematically protected. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 requires the most effective leaders to build a safeguarding culture embedded in governance, policy, and daily practice. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 places ultimate responsibility for student safety with the school principal. At this level, you are accountable for the school's safeguarding — and you lead that accountability with rigour and care.
keyTakeaways: Design and maintain the school's whole safeguarding policy, training system, and governance framework, ensuring full compliance with Indonesian law and Cambridge requirements.
Build and maintain relationships with external child protection authorities and ensure the school responds correctly to referrals and external investigations.
Mentor other leaders to develop their safeguarding governance skills and evaluate the strength of the school's safeguarding culture over time.
selfAssessment: I can design and maintain a whole-school safeguarding policy and governance framework that meets Indonesian legal requirements and Cambridge's safeguarding expectations.
I can build and maintain productive relationships with Indonesian child protection authorities and ensure the school responds appropriately to any external referrals or investigations.
I can design and oversee a whole-school safeguarding training programme that ensures all staff — including new staff — understand their responsibilities.
I can mentor two or more leaders to develop their safeguarding governance skills using structured coaching and evidence-based feedback.
I can evaluate the strength of the school's safeguarding culture annually using audit data, incident records, and staff survey evidence, and present findings to the board.

activity.task: Review and revise the school's whole-school safeguarding policy and governance framework. Conduct an annual safeguarding culture review and mentor two leaders in their safeguarding responsibilities.
activity.output: A revised whole-school safeguarding policy and governance framework document (4–6 pages) including: policy principles, training requirements, reporting structures, record-keeping protocols, external partnership framework, and annual audit process. An annual safeguarding culture review summary (1 page). Mentoring logs from two structured sessions.
activity.evidence: Final policy and framework; annual safeguarding culture review summary; mentoring logs; record of board or senior leadership presentation.

practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, you actively contribute to building a positive school climate and a strong sense of community identity within your team or department. School climate refers to the quality of relationships, the sense of safety, the shared values, and the feeling of belonging that people experience in the school. At this level, you model the school's values in your own leadership behaviour and encourage teachers in your team to do the same. You create department routines and activities that reinforce community identity and help students feel they belong. You also listen to students and staff about what makes them feel valued or excluded, and you act on what you hear. PSEL Standard 8 calls on leaders to engage the school community in building a positive culture grounded in shared values. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 requires leaders to promote and sustain a positive school ethos. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies the creation of a positive school work culture as a core managerial competency for school principals. At this stage, you build the school's climate from the inside — through your daily behaviour and the culture of your team.
keyTakeaways: Model the school's values consistently in your own leadership behaviour and expect teachers in your team to do the same.
Create department routines and activities that reinforce community identity and help students feel they belong.
Listen actively to students and staff about what shapes their experience of the school's climate and act on what you learn.
selfAssessment: I can describe the school's core values and give two or three specific examples of how I model these values in my daily leadership behaviour.
I can design or maintain at least one department routine or activity that reinforces community identity and student belonging.
I can gather feedback from students and staff in my team about the school climate and use it to make at least one change to department practice.
I can facilitate a department conversation about shared values that results in agreed expectations for how teachers and students treat each other.
I can identify a threat to positive climate in my area — such as a persistent behaviour issue or a breakdown in team relationships — and lead an effective response.

activity.task: Design and implement a team-level climate and values review in your department. Gather feedback from students and teachers and produce a short action plan to strengthen community identity and belonging.
activity.output: A department climate action plan (1–2 pages) with: feedback summary, two or three specific climate strengths, two or three areas for improvement, and planned actions with timelines.
activity.evidence: Completed climate action plan; feedback collection evidence such as a survey or meeting notes; record of at least one action implemented.

advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, you lead school climate development across a programme or phase. You design and facilitate processes that build a shared sense of values and community identity across multiple departments. You analyse climate data — including student and staff surveys, behaviour records, and attendance patterns — to evaluate the quality of the school's climate and identify where it needs strengthening. You work with student leadership groups, parent communities, and cultural representatives to ensure community identity is genuinely inclusive. You also address climate challenges — such as bullying, disengagement, or cultural conflict — at a systemic level. PSEL Standard 8 calls on leaders to engage the school community in building a shared culture of care, respect, and high expectations. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 requires leaders to sustain a school ethos that reflects shared values and serves all community members equitably. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 includes the promotion of a positive school work culture as a principal-level responsibility. At this level, school climate is something you shape intentionally — through data, strategy, and sustained leadership.
keyTakeaways: Analyse climate data — including student and staff surveys, behaviour records, and attendance — to evaluate the quality of the school's climate and identify priorities.
Design and facilitate processes that build shared values and community identity across multiple departments or year groups.
Address climate challenges at a systemic level by involving students, families, and cultural community representatives in the response.
selfAssessment: I can analyse student and staff survey data, behaviour records, and attendance trends to evaluate the quality of school climate across my phase.
I can design and lead a cross-departmental process — such as a values programme, a climate improvement initiative, or a student leadership project — that strengthens community identity.
I can work with student leaders, parent representatives, and cultural community members to ensure the school's community identity is genuinely inclusive and representative.
I can address a systemic climate challenge — such as bullying patterns, chronic disengagement, or inter-group conflict — with a structured and evidence-based leadership response.
I can produce a phase-level school climate review report for senior leadership that summarises data findings and recommends systemic improvements.

activity.task: Lead a phase-level school climate review. Collect survey and behavioural data, facilitate a consultation with student leaders or parent representatives, and produce a climate improvement plan.
activity.output: A phase-level school climate review and improvement plan (3–4 pages) covering: data summary, consultation findings, current strengths, priority improvement areas, and recommended actions.
activity.evidence: Completed climate review and improvement plan; data collected; record of student or parent consultation; evidence of at least one improvement action initiated.

lead.reading: At the Lead/Mentor level, you design and steward the school's whole approach to school climate, values, and community identity as a strategic commitment. You create the frameworks, rituals, policies, and leadership practices that shape the school's culture over time. You ensure that the school's values are visible, consistently lived, and understood by all members of the community — including students, staff, families, and external partners. You mentor other leaders to develop their climate leadership skills. You also evaluate the school's climate over time using longitudinal data and present findings to the board. PSEL Standard 8 calls on lead leaders to build and sustain a school culture that is inclusive, caring, and grounded in shared values. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 requires the most effective leaders to create a school ethos that defines the institution's identity and is sustained beyond any single leader's tenure. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 places responsibility for school culture with the school principal. At this level, the school's culture is your most important leadership legacy.
keyTakeaways: Design the school's whole culture framework — including values, rituals, community norms, and climate monitoring system — as a long-term strategic commitment.
Mentor other leaders to develop climate leadership skills and sustain the school's culture consistently across all areas.
Evaluate the school's climate over time using longitudinal data and present trend findings to the board as evidence of sustained community wellbeing.
selfAssessment: I can design a whole-school culture and values framework that defines the school's identity, expected behaviours, community norms, and climate monitoring process.
I can create school-wide rituals, traditions, and structures that reinforce the school's values and make all community members feel they belong.
I can mentor two or more leaders to develop their climate leadership skills and ensure the school's culture is sustained consistently across all areas.
I can evaluate the school's climate over time using longitudinal student, staff, and family survey data and present trend findings to the board.
I can represent the school's culture and community identity to Cambridge networks, external partners, and regulatory bodies as a source of institutional strength.

activity.task: Design or significantly revise the school's whole-school culture and values framework. Conduct a longitudinal climate review using data from at least two years and mentor two leaders in their climate leadership.
activity.output: A whole-school culture and values framework document (3–5 pages) including: values statement, community norms, key rituals and structures, climate monitoring system, and leadership responsibilities. A one-page longitudinal climate review summary with trend data. Mentoring logs from two structured sessions.
activity.evidence: Final framework document; longitudinal climate data summary; mentoring logs; record of board or senior leadership presentation.

awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, you are beginning to understand what it means for your school to operate as a Cambridge centre and what compliance and accreditation involve. A Cambridge centre is a school approved by Cambridge Assessment International Education to deliver Cambridge programmes such as Primary, Lower Secondary, IGCSE, or A Level. Compliance means meeting Cambridge's operational requirements — including examination administration, candidate registration, results reporting, and quality assurance. Accreditation is a formal review process through which Cambridge evaluates whether a centre is meeting its standards. At this level, you learn what Cambridge requires of your school, who is responsible for compliance, and what the main risks of non-compliance are. PSEL Standard 9 states that effective leaders ensure the organisation operates in accordance with all applicable requirements. Cambridge School Leader Standard 3 requires leaders to understand and apply the standards set by Cambridge for centre operation and school evaluation. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies regulatory compliance as a core managerial competency for school principals. At this stage, your goal is to understand the Cambridge compliance landscape before you begin to lead within it.
keyTakeaways: Understand what it means to be a Cambridge centre and what the key compliance requirements are for your school.
Know who holds Cambridge compliance responsibilities in your school and how those responsibilities are organised.
Begin identifying the risks of non-compliance and how they could affect your students and school.
selfAssessment: I can explain what it means to be a Cambridge centre and describe the key Cambridge programmes delivered at my school.
I can identify the main Cambridge compliance requirements for my school — including examination administration, candidate registration, and results reporting.
I can describe who holds Cambridge compliance responsibilities in my school and how these are divided between staff.
I can explain what PSEL Standard 9 says about ensuring the school operates in compliance with applicable requirements.
I can identify two or three risks that could arise from non-compliance with Cambridge centre requirements.

activity.task: Review your school's Cambridge centre compliance documentation — including the Cambridge Centre Handbook, examination administration procedures, and candidate registration records. Write a short compliance review summary identifying areas of strength and any gaps.
activity.output: A one-page compliance review summary with: key compliance areas reviewed, two or three strengths identified, and two or three areas where compliance procedures could be improved or better documented.
activity.evidence: Completed compliance review summary; Cambridge documentation reviewed; record of brief discussion with the Cambridge centre coordinator or examinations officer about the findings.

practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, you apply Cambridge centre compliance requirements consistently in your area of responsibility. You understand the Cambridge examination cycle — from candidate entry to results despatch — and ensure that your team meets all deadlines and procedural requirements. You keep accurate records, follow Cambridge's candidate registration and examination administration procedures, and respond promptly to Cambridge communications and updates. You also understand what Cambridge expects during a formal centre review or accreditation visit and help prepare your area accordingly. PSEL Standard 9 calls on leaders to ensure the school operates efficiently and in compliance with external requirements. Cambridge School Leader Standard 3 requires leaders to engage actively with Cambridge's quality assurance and evaluation processes. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 includes compliance with educational regulatory bodies as a key managerial responsibility of school leaders. At this stage, compliance is not a last-minute activity — it is a structured, ongoing practice you lead in your area with discipline and accuracy.
keyTakeaways: Ensure your team meets all Cambridge examination cycle deadlines — from candidate entry through to results processing — accurately and on time.
Maintain accurate Cambridge compliance records in your area and respond promptly to Cambridge communications and updates.
Understand what Cambridge expects during a formal centre review or accreditation visit and prepare your area accordingly.
selfAssessment: I can manage the Cambridge examination cycle in my area, ensuring all deadlines for candidate entry, examination administration, and results processing are met.
I can maintain accurate and up-to-date Cambridge compliance records, including candidate registration data and examination administration logs.
I can respond to Cambridge communications — including updates to the Cambridge Handbook or changes to examination procedures — and implement required changes in my area.
I can prepare my area for a Cambridge centre review by organising evidence of compliance with Cambridge's key operational requirements.
I can identify a compliance risk in my area and describe the steps I would take to address it before it becomes a problem.

activity.task: Conduct a compliance readiness review for your area ahead of a Cambridge examination session or centre review. Identify any gaps and produce a compliance action plan.
activity.output: A compliance action plan (1–2 pages) listing: compliance areas reviewed, gaps or risks identified, actions required, responsible persons, and deadlines for each action.
activity.evidence: Completed compliance action plan; evidence of Cambridge documentation reviewed; record of actions taken to address any gaps found.

advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, you lead Cambridge centre compliance across a programme or phase. You coordinate compliance activities across multiple departments or year groups, ensuring all Cambridge requirements are met consistently and that examination administration is accurate, secure, and timely. You also lead the school's preparation for formal Cambridge accreditation or centre reviews — gathering evidence, facilitating self-evaluation, and producing structured reports. You analyse compliance data across your phase to identify systemic risks and design preventive responses. PSEL Standard 9 requires leaders to build operational systems that are efficient, transparent, and aligned to external regulatory requirements. Cambridge School Leader Standard 3 calls on leaders to lead self-evaluation and external review processes with rigour and honesty. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 places responsibility for institutional compliance and accreditation with the school principal. At this level, your leadership ensures Cambridge compliance is a coordinated, whole-phase function — not a collection of individual responsibilities managed in isolation.
keyTakeaways: Coordinate Cambridge compliance activities across multiple departments or year groups, ensuring consistency and accuracy across the whole phase.
Lead the school's preparation for Cambridge accreditation or centre reviews, including evidence gathering, self-evaluation, and report production.
Analyse compliance data across your phase to identify systemic risks and design preventive responses before problems arise.
selfAssessment: I can coordinate the Cambridge examination cycle across multiple departments or year groups, ensuring all teams meet their compliance obligations accurately and on time.
I can lead the preparation of evidence for a Cambridge centre review or accreditation visit, working with department heads and the examinations team.
I can analyse compliance data across my phase — including entry accuracy, late submissions, and examination irregularities — and produce a risk report for senior leadership.
I can facilitate a pre-accreditation self-evaluation process that results in a structured report identifying strengths and areas for improvement in Cambridge compliance.
I can design briefing sessions to strengthen Cambridge compliance knowledge and practice across departments in my phase.

activity.task: Lead the preparation of your phase for a Cambridge centre review or examination session compliance audit. Gather evidence across at least three departments, facilitate a review meeting, and produce a compliance readiness report.
activity.output: A compliance readiness report (2–3 pages) covering: compliance areas assessed, evidence gathered, strengths identified, risks or gaps found, and actions required before the review or examination session.
activity.evidence: Completed compliance readiness report; evidence gathered from departments; record of review meeting; actions taken in response to gaps identified.

lead.reading: At the Lead/Mentor level, you design and steward the school's whole Cambridge compliance and accreditation system. You create the policies, procedures, roles, and monitoring systems that ensure the school meets all Cambridge centre requirements consistently. You represent the school in formal Cambridge accreditation processes — including hosting Cambridge visits and responding to accreditation recommendations. You build and maintain a strong institutional relationship with Cambridge through the centre coordinator role and broader engagement. You mentor other leaders to develop their Cambridge compliance knowledge and leadership capacity. PSEL Standard 9 calls on lead leaders to build systems that ensure the school operates efficiently, transparently, and in compliance with all external requirements. Cambridge School Leader Standard 3 requires the most effective leaders to embed self-evaluation and compliance as structural features of school governance. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 places ultimate accountability for regulatory compliance with the school principal. At this level, Cambridge compliance is a governance function — and you lead it as such.
keyTakeaways: Design the school's whole Cambridge compliance and accreditation system, including policies, roles, monitoring processes, and annual review cycles.
Represent the school in formal Cambridge accreditation processes and maintain a strong, productive institutional relationship with Cambridge.
Mentor other leaders to develop Cambridge compliance knowledge and embed a compliance-conscious culture across the school.
selfAssessment: I can design a whole-school Cambridge compliance system with clear policies, assigned roles, monitoring processes, and an annual review cycle.
I can represent the school effectively in formal Cambridge accreditation visits, including preparing documentation, hosting evaluators, and responding to accreditation recommendations.
I can maintain a strong institutional relationship with Cambridge through regular centre coordinator engagement, participation in Cambridge networks, and timely communication.
I can mentor two or more leaders to develop their Cambridge compliance knowledge and responsibilities, using structured coaching and documented progress reviews.
I can evaluate the school's overall Cambridge compliance performance annually and present findings and improvement actions to the school board.

activity.task: Design or significantly revise the school's whole Cambridge compliance and accreditation management system. Lead a mock accreditation review and mentor two leaders in their compliance responsibilities.
activity.output: A whole-school Cambridge compliance and accreditation management document (4–6 pages) including: compliance policy, roles and responsibilities, annual compliance calendar, monitoring process, and accreditation preparation guide. A mock accreditation summary report (1 page). Mentoring logs from two structured sessions.
activity.evidence: Final compliance management document; mock accreditation summary; mentoring logs; record of board or senior leadership presentation.

awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, you are beginning to understand the Indonesian regulatory framework for education and how it applies to your school. Schools in Indonesia — including international and Cambridge schools — must comply with national regulations issued by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Research, and Technology through Permendikbud regulations. These cover areas such as curriculum requirements, school accreditation through BAN-S/M, teacher certification, student enrolment, and data reporting. At this level, you learn which Permendikbud regulations are most relevant to your school, who is responsible for regulatory compliance, and what the consequences of non-compliance could be. PSEL Standard 9 states that effective leaders ensure the school operates in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. Cambridge School Leader Standard 3 calls on leaders to understand the external regulatory environment in which their school operates. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 is itself a foundational regulatory document — establishing the competency standards required of all school principals in Indonesia. At this stage, your focus is on building regulatory knowledge before you begin to lead compliance in your area.
keyTakeaways: Understand which Permendikbud regulations are most relevant to your school and what they require in terms of curriculum, accreditation, and reporting.
Know who holds Indonesian regulatory compliance responsibilities in your school and how these are organised.
Begin identifying the risks of non-compliance with Indonesian educational regulations and their potential consequences.
selfAssessment: I can identify the key Permendikbud regulations that apply to my school and explain what each one requires.
I can describe who holds Indonesian regulatory compliance responsibilities in my school and how these are distributed across leadership roles.
I can explain how Indonesian national curriculum requirements interact with the Cambridge curriculum at my school.
I can explain what Permendiknas No. 13/2007 establishes and why it is relevant to my role as a school leader.
I can identify two or three consequences of non-compliance with Indonesian educational regulations that could affect my school.

activity.task: Review the key Permendikbud regulations that apply to your school. Write a short regulatory landscape summary for your team or department, explaining what each regulation requires and how your area currently meets those requirements.
activity.output: A one-page regulatory landscape summary listing: the three most relevant regulations for your area, what each requires, and whether your area currently has the documentation or processes to demonstrate compliance.
activity.evidence: Completed regulatory landscape summary; regulatory documents reviewed; record of brief discussion with the school principal or compliance lead about the findings.

practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, you apply Indonesian regulatory compliance requirements consistently in your area of responsibility. You understand the BAN-S/M accreditation process and what your school needs to maintain or improve its accreditation status. You ensure that your area has accurate and current documentation to demonstrate compliance with relevant Permendikbud requirements — including curriculum documentation, teacher data, and student enrolment records. You also respond to regulatory updates promptly and adjust your area's practices when requirements change. PSEL Standard 9 calls on leaders to ensure the school meets all applicable regulatory requirements and operates with transparency. Cambridge School Leader Standard 3 requires leaders to understand and respond to the external regulatory environment. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 establishes the managerial competency standards that all Indonesian school leaders must meet — including regulatory compliance. At this stage, Indonesian regulatory compliance is a structured, ongoing practice in your area — not something you address only when an inspection is imminent.
keyTakeaways: Maintain accurate and current documentation in your area to demonstrate compliance with relevant Permendikbud regulations.
Understand the BAN-S/M accreditation process and ensure your area's practices and records support the school's accreditation status.
Respond promptly to regulatory updates and adjust your area's procedures when Indonesian requirements change.
selfAssessment: I can maintain accurate and current documentation in my area that demonstrates compliance with the Permendikbud regulations relevant to my role.
I can explain the BAN-S/M accreditation process and describe how my area contributes to the school's accreditation evidence.
I can identify when a Permendikbud regulation has been updated and take the necessary steps to adjust my area's practices accordingly.
I can prepare my area for a regulatory inspection or BAN-S/M accreditation visit by organising required documentation and evidence.
I can explain to teachers in my team why Indonesian regulatory compliance matters and what their individual responsibilities are.

activity.task: Conduct a regulatory compliance check in your area, focusing on the three most relevant Permendikbud requirements. Identify any gaps and produce a compliance improvement plan.
activity.output: A regulatory compliance improvement plan (1–2 pages) listing: regulations reviewed, current compliance status for each, gaps or weaknesses identified, and actions required with timelines and responsible persons.
