activity.evidence: Completed compliance improvement plan; regulatory documents reviewed; evidence of documentation updated or procedures adjusted as a result.

advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, you lead Indonesian regulatory compliance across a programme or phase. You coordinate compliance activities across multiple departments, ensuring consistent documentation and accurate reporting to support the school's BAN-S/M accreditation. You lead preparation for regulatory inspections and accreditation reviews — including facilitating self-evaluation, gathering evidence, and producing structured compliance reports. You monitor regulatory changes and design whole-phase responses when requirements are updated. You also communicate compliance expectations clearly to department heads and support them to fulfil their own regulatory responsibilities. PSEL Standard 9 requires leaders to build operational systems that ensure transparent, efficient, and compliant school operations at scale. Cambridge School Leader Standard 3 calls on leaders to engage rigorously with self-evaluation and external review processes, including regulatory reviews. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 places whole-school regulatory compliance oversight as a principal-level responsibility. At this level, your leadership ensures that Indonesian regulatory compliance is coordinated, comprehensive, and current across your whole phase.
keyTakeaways: Coordinate Indonesian regulatory compliance activities across multiple departments, ensuring consistent documentation and accurate reporting.
Lead preparation for BAN-S/M accreditation reviews and regulatory inspections, including evidence gathering and structured self-evaluation.
Monitor changes to Permendikbud regulations and design whole-phase responses to ensure requirements are met promptly.
selfAssessment: I can coordinate Indonesian regulatory compliance activities across multiple departments, ensuring all areas maintain current and accurate documentation.
I can lead the preparation of evidence for a BAN-S/M accreditation review, working with department heads to organise required documentation and self-evaluation materials.
I can monitor changes to Permendikbud regulations and design implementation plans to ensure my phase meets updated requirements on time.
I can produce a phase-level regulatory compliance report for senior leadership that identifies strengths, gaps, and recommended actions.
I can facilitate a pre-accreditation self-evaluation meeting across departments that results in an honest, structured assessment of compliance readiness.

activity.task: Lead a phase-level BAN-S/M accreditation readiness review. Gather compliance evidence across at least three departments, facilitate a review meeting, and produce an accreditation readiness report.
activity.output: A BAN-S/M accreditation readiness report (2–3 pages) covering: compliance areas assessed, evidence gathered per department, strengths identified, gaps or risks found, and a prioritised action plan.
activity.evidence: Completed accreditation readiness report; evidence gathered from departments; record of review meeting; actions taken to address gaps identified.

lead.reading: At the Lead/Mentor level, you design and steward the school's whole Indonesian regulatory compliance system. You create the policies, procedures, and monitoring structures that ensure the school meets all Permendikbud requirements consistently and maintains strong BAN-S/M accreditation status. You represent the school in formal regulatory inspections and accreditation reviews — including hosting BAN-S/M evaluators and responding formally to accreditation findings. You mentor other leaders in compliance knowledge and leadership. PSEL Standard 9 calls on lead leaders to build governance structures that ensure full, transparent compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. Cambridge School Leader Standard 3 requires the most effective leaders to manage external accountability processes with rigour and integrity. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 is the foundational standard for principal accountability in Indonesia — and at this level, you embody and lead compliance with it across the whole school. At this level, regulatory compliance is a governance priority embedded in how the school is led and managed every day.
keyTakeaways: Design the school's whole Indonesian regulatory compliance system, including policies, roles, monitoring calendar, and accreditation management process.
Represent the school credibly in formal BAN-S/M accreditation reviews and regulatory inspections, and respond effectively to accreditation findings.
Mentor other leaders to develop regulatory compliance knowledge and build a culture where compliance is understood and owned across the school.
selfAssessment: I can design a whole-school Indonesian regulatory compliance system with clear policies, assigned roles, a compliance calendar, and an annual review process.
I can represent the school in formal BAN-S/M accreditation processes — including hosting evaluators, presenting evidence, and responding formally to accreditation findings.
I can ensure the school's compliance documentation is accurate, current, and organised to support both BAN-S/M accreditation and Cambridge centre requirements simultaneously.
I can mentor two or more leaders to develop their regulatory compliance knowledge and leadership skills, using structured coaching and documented progress reviews.
I can evaluate the school's overall Indonesian regulatory compliance status annually and present findings and recommended actions to the school board.

activity.task: Design or significantly revise the school's whole Indonesian regulatory compliance and accreditation management system. Lead a mock BAN-S/M review and mentor two leaders in their compliance responsibilities.
activity.output: A whole-school Indonesian regulatory compliance and accreditation management document (4–6 pages) including: compliance policy, roles and responsibilities, annual compliance calendar, BAN-S/M preparation process, and evidence management guide. A mock BAN-S/M review summary (1 page). Mentoring logs from two structured sessions.
activity.evidence: Final compliance management document; mock review summary; mentoring logs; record of board or senior leadership presentation.

practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, you apply ethical principles consistently in your leadership decisions and hold yourself and your team accountable for their actions. Ethical decision-making means making choices that are fair, honest, and in the best interests of students and the school community — even when the right choice is difficult. At this level, you recognise ethical dilemmas when they arise and use a structured approach to work through them. You are also transparent and accountable in your leadership — you do what you say you will do, and you acknowledge mistakes honestly. PSEL Standard 2 calls on leaders to act with personal integrity, model ethical professional norms, and hold themselves and others to the same standard. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 requires leaders to demonstrate honesty, fairness, and integrity in all aspects of their work. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies personal integrity and ethical professional conduct as foundational competencies for school principals. At this stage, ethical leadership is not a set of rules you follow — it is a consistent practice that others in your team can observe and rely on.
keyTakeaways: Recognise ethical dilemmas in your leadership context and use a structured approach to work through them honestly and fairly.
Be transparent and accountable in your own leadership — following through on commitments and acknowledging mistakes openly.
Model ethical professional conduct consistently so that your team can observe and rely on your integrity.
selfAssessment: I can identify an ethical dilemma I have faced in my leadership role and describe the approach I used to work through it.
I can describe a structured framework for ethical decision-making and explain how I would apply it to a difficult leadership situation.
I can demonstrate accountability in my own leadership by following through on commitments, meeting deadlines, and acknowledging mistakes honestly.
I can address a situation where a member of my team has acted in a way that falls below the expected ethical standard, using a fair and constructive process.
I can explain what PSEL Standard 2 says about professional integrity and how it applies to my current role as a school leader.

activity.task: Write a personal ethical leadership reflection — describing an ethical dilemma you have faced or observed in your school, how it was resolved, and what you learned about ethical decision-making from the experience.
activity.output: A written ethical leadership reflection (1–2 pages) covering: the dilemma described clearly, the decision-making process used, the outcome, and key lessons for your own leadership practice.
activity.evidence: Completed reflection document; record of a structured conversation with a mentor or trusted colleague about the reflection and its implications for your practice.

advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, you lead ethical decision-making and accountability across a programme or phase. You help other leaders and teachers recognise and respond to ethical challenges in their own practice. You create systems and norms in your phase that promote transparency, fairness, and honest communication. You also lead accountability processes — including difficult conversations, performance management, and the management of complaints or concerns — with fairness and professionalism. When ethical challenges involve multiple stakeholders, you manage them with sensitivity and clarity. PSEL Standard 2 requires leaders to hold the whole school community to ethical standards — not just to model integrity personally. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 calls on leaders to create a school culture where ethical conduct is expected and consistently upheld. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies the ability to lead with personal and institutional integrity as a principal-level competency. At this level, ethical leadership is a systemic commitment — embedded in how your phase operates and how leaders make decisions.
keyTakeaways: Create systems and norms in your phase that promote transparency, fairness, and ethical professional conduct at all levels.
Lead accountability processes — including difficult conversations and performance concerns — with fairness, professionalism, and respect.
Support other leaders and teachers to recognise and respond to ethical challenges in their own practice.
selfAssessment: I can identify ethical risks in my phase — such as unfair practices, lack of transparency, or conflicts of interest — and design responses that address them systematically.
I can lead a difficult accountability conversation with a teacher or leader that is fair, evidence-based, and focused on improvement rather than blame.
I can manage a complaint or concern raised by a parent, student, or staff member in a way that is transparent, confidential, and procedurally correct.
I can facilitate a team discussion about professional ethics that results in shared expectations and agreed standards of conduct.
I can produce an accountability review report for senior leadership that evaluates the ethical culture and accountability practices in my phase, with recommendations for improvement.

activity.task: Conduct an ethical culture and accountability review in your phase. Gather evidence through staff surveys or structured conversations and produce a review report with recommendations.
activity.output: An ethical culture and accountability review report (2–3 pages) covering: review methodology, key findings about ethical practice and accountability in your phase, strengths, areas for development, and recommended actions.
activity.evidence: Completed review report; evidence gathered such as survey results or conversation notes; record of follow-up actions taken; record of report shared with senior leadership.

lead.reading: At the Lead/Mentor level, you design and lead the school's ethical governance framework and accountability culture. You create the policies, processes, and leadership norms that ensure the school operates with integrity at every level. You handle complex ethical challenges — including safeguarding concerns, conflicts of interest, whistleblowing disclosures, and external complaints — with professional confidence and legal awareness. You mentor other leaders to develop their ethical decision-making skills and build accountability capacity across the school. You also ensure the school's ethical standards are visible to external stakeholders — including Cambridge, government bodies, and the parent community. PSEL Standard 2 calls on lead leaders to build an ethical, accountable school culture that is embedded in all structures and practices. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 requires the most effective leaders to model and sustain a culture of integrity and professional responsibility. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 places ultimate responsibility for institutional integrity with the school principal. At this level, the school's ethical culture is a leadership legacy you are actively building and protecting.
keyTakeaways: Design the school's ethical governance framework and accountability policy, ensuring integrity is embedded in all school structures and decisions.
Handle complex ethical challenges — including whistleblowing, conflicts of interest, and external complaints — with professional confidence, fairness, and legal awareness.
Mentor other leaders to develop ethical decision-making skills and build a school-wide culture of accountability and professional integrity.
selfAssessment: I can design a whole-school ethical governance framework that includes a code of conduct, conflict of interest policy, accountability processes, and whistleblowing procedures.
I can manage a complex ethical challenge — such as a whistleblowing disclosure, an external complaint, or a conflict of interest — with fairness, confidentiality, and legal awareness.
I can mentor two or more leaders to develop their ethical decision-making skills using structured coaching, case-based learning, and reflective practice.
I can evaluate the school's ethical culture annually using survey data, accountability records, and stakeholder feedback, and present findings to the board.
I can represent the school's ethical governance and accountability standards to Cambridge, government inspectors, and the parent community with confidence and evidence.

activity.task: Design or significantly revise the school's ethical governance framework and accountability policy. Facilitate a leadership ethics workshop and mentor two leaders in ethical decision-making.
activity.output: A whole-school ethical governance framework document (3–5 pages) including: ethical principles, code of conduct, accountability processes, whistleblowing procedures, and conflict of interest policy. A summary record of the leadership ethics workshop. Mentoring logs from two structured sessions.
activity.evidence: Final governance framework document; workshop summary record; mentoring logs; record of board or senior leadership presentation.

practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, you manage operational responsibilities and resources within your team or department efficiently and transparently. Operational management at this level includes managing budgets, timetables, physical resources, and administrative processes in your area. It also means using school resources — including time, money, and staff — wisely and in the best interests of students. You plan ahead, track expenditure accurately, and communicate resource needs to senior leadership clearly and with evidence. PSEL Standard 9 calls on leaders to ensure the school's operations are managed efficiently and transparently in service of the educational mission. Cambridge School Leader Standard 5 requires leaders to manage resources and operations in ways that support staff and student learning. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies operational and administrative management as a core managerial competency for school principals. At this stage, your operational leadership ensures your department runs smoothly and that resources are used purposefully — not wasted or mismanaged.
keyTakeaways: Manage departmental resources — including budget, time, and physical resources — efficiently and transparently.
Plan operational activities in your department ahead of time and communicate resource needs to senior leadership clearly and with evidence.
Ensure that resource decisions in your area are made in the best interests of students and are aligned to school priorities.
selfAssessment: I can manage a departmental budget accurately, including tracking expenditure, prioritising spending, and reporting to senior leadership.
I can plan operational activities in my department ahead of time — including timetabling, resource procurement, and event coordination — and adjust plans when circumstances change.
I can make resource decisions in my area that are transparent, evidence-based, and clearly linked to student learning priorities.
I can communicate resource needs to senior leadership clearly, using evidence to justify requests and showing awareness of the school's overall financial context.
I can identify an inefficiency in my department's operations and design a practical solution that improves the use of time, money, or physical resources.

activity.task: Conduct an operational efficiency review in your department. Identify one area where resources — including time, budget, or materials — are being used inefficiently and design a practical improvement plan.
activity.output: A one-page operational improvement plan with: the inefficiency identified, evidence gathered, proposed solution, expected impact, implementation steps, and timeline.
activity.evidence: Completed operational improvement plan; evidence of the issue identified; record of at least one practical change made in response.

advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, you lead operational management and resource stewardship across a programme or phase. You coordinate operational planning across multiple departments, ensuring resources are allocated fairly and used efficiently. You analyse operational data — including budget reports, space utilisation, and staff allocation — to evaluate whether resources are deployed in the most effective way. You also manage cross-departmental operational challenges — such as timetable conflicts, shared space allocation, or budget shortfalls — with fairness and strategic thinking. PSEL Standard 9 requires leaders to build operational systems that are efficient, transparent, and aligned to the school's educational mission. Cambridge School Leader Standard 5 calls on leaders to ensure operational management supports — and does not constrain — teaching and learning. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 includes whole-school operational management as a principal-level responsibility. At this level, your operational leadership ensures resources across your phase are deployed strategically — supporting school priorities, not just managing daily logistics.
keyTakeaways: Coordinate operational planning and resource allocation across multiple departments, ensuring fairness and strategic alignment to school priorities.
Analyse operational data — including budget reports, space use, and staff deployment — to evaluate whether resources are being used as effectively as possible.
Manage cross-departmental operational challenges with fairness, transparency, and strategic thinking.
selfAssessment: I can coordinate operational planning and budget allocation across at least three departments in my phase, ensuring resources are distributed fairly and in line with school priorities.
I can analyse operational data — including budget performance, space utilisation, and staff time allocation — to identify inefficiencies and recommend improvements.
I can manage a cross-departmental operational challenge — such as a timetable conflict or budget shortfall — using a fair and transparent process that minimises disruption to teaching and learning.
I can produce an operational performance review report for senior leadership covering budget use, resource deployment, and operational improvements achieved across my phase.
I can build the operational management capacity of department heads in my phase through structured coaching and shared planning processes.

activity.task: Conduct a phase-level operational performance review covering budget use and resource deployment across at least three departments. Produce a report with findings and recommendations.
activity.output: A phase-level operational performance review report (2–3 pages) covering: budget summary, resource deployment analysis, operational strengths and inefficiencies identified, and three or more recommendations for improvement.
activity.evidence: Completed operational performance review report; budget and resource data used; record of discussion with department heads about findings; evidence of at least one operational improvement implemented.

lead.reading: At the Lead/Mentor level, you design and steward the school's whole operational management and resource stewardship system. You create the policies, financial management frameworks, and planning processes that ensure the school's resources — including budget, staff time, facilities, and technology — are used wisely, transparently, and in the best interests of students. You ensure that operational management is aligned to the school's strategic priorities and that financial decisions are made with integrity and accountability. You mentor other leaders to develop their operational management skills and build a culture of responsible stewardship. PSEL Standard 9 calls on lead leaders to ensure the school's operations and resources are managed transparently, efficiently, and in full service of the educational mission. Cambridge School Leader Standard 5 requires the most effective leaders to build operational systems that are sustainable and that support long-term school development. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 places ultimate accountability for school operations and finances with the school principal. At this level, operational excellence is a strategic leadership responsibility that directly enables your school's educational mission.
keyTakeaways: Design the school's whole operational management and resource stewardship system, including financial policies, planning processes, and accountability mechanisms.
Ensure all financial decisions are made with integrity, transparency, and clear alignment to the school's strategic and educational priorities.
Mentor other leaders to develop operational management skills and build a school-wide culture of responsible and transparent resource stewardship.
selfAssessment: I can design a whole-school operational management framework that includes financial policies, resource allocation processes, planning cycles, and accountability mechanisms.
I can ensure that the school's financial decisions are made transparently, with clear records, appropriate authorisation processes, and alignment to strategic priorities.
I can present the school's operational and financial performance to the school board — including budget performance, resource utilisation, and planned investments — in a clear and evidence-based way.
I can mentor two or more leaders to develop their operational management and resource stewardship skills using structured coaching and practical problem-solving conversations.
I can evaluate the efficiency and integrity of the school's operational management system annually and recommend systemic improvements to the board.

activity.task: Design or significantly revise the school's whole-school operational management and resource stewardship framework. Present the framework to the board and mentor two leaders in operational management.
activity.output: A whole-school operational management and resource stewardship framework document (4–6 pages) including: financial management policy, resource allocation principles, planning and procurement processes, accountability mechanisms, and an annual operational review cycle. Mentoring logs from two structured sessions. A brief board presentation summary (1 page).
activity.evidence: Final framework document; board presentation summary; mentoring logs; evidence of at least one operational system change made as a result of the review.

awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, you are beginning to understand what meaningful family engagement means and why parent communication is a leadership responsibility. Research consistently shows that students achieve better outcomes when their families are engaged in their education. Family engagement is not just about sending newsletters or holding annual report nights — it means building relationships of trust, keeping families well informed, and involving them in the school community. At this level, you learn how your school communicates with families, what channels and formats are used, and how communication practices can either include or exclude different families. PSEL Standard 8 states that effective leaders engage families as partners in education, creating opportunities for genuine and meaningful participation. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 emphasises that leaders must build strong, inclusive relationships with all members of the school community. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies community and family engagement as a core managerial competency for school principals. At this stage, your focus is on understanding how your school currently communicates with families and beginning to identify where the practice could be stronger.
keyTakeaways: Understand how your school communicates with families — including what channels, formats, and languages are used.
Recognise that meaningful family engagement goes beyond information-sharing — it builds trust and involves families as active partners.
Begin identifying which families in your area may not be fully reached by current communication practices.
selfAssessment: I can describe how my school communicates with families, including the main channels, formats, and frequency used.
I can identify one or two groups of families in my area who may not be fully reached by current communication practices and explain why.
I can explain the difference between informing families and meaningfully engaging them as partners in education.
I can explain what PSEL Standard 8 says about the meaningful engagement of families and communities.
I can give an example of a communication practice in my department that could be improved to better reach all families.

activity.task: Review the family communication practices in your department or year group — including the channels used, the language and tone of messages, and the frequency of contact. Write a short analysis identifying what is working well and what could be improved.
activity.output: A one-page family communication review with: current practices described, two or three strengths, two or three areas for improvement, and one practical action your team can take next term.
activity.evidence: Completed communication review document; examples of current family communications reviewed; record of brief discussion with a colleague or mentor about the findings.

practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, you engage families meaningfully and communicate with parents consistently and effectively within your team or department. You ensure that communication is clear, respectful, timely, and accessible — including for families whose first language is not English or Indonesian. You create opportunities for families to engage with the school beyond receiving information — for example through curriculum evenings, student-led conferences, or parent feedback sessions. You also respond promptly and professionally to parent concerns and questions. PSEL Standard 8 calls on leaders to create meaningful opportunities for families to participate in school life and to be treated as genuine partners. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 requires leaders to build strong, respectful relationships with all members of the school community. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies community and family engagement as a key managerial responsibility for school leaders. At this stage, family engagement is a regular, structured part of how your department operates — not something that happens only when problems arise.
keyTakeaways: Ensure department communication with families is clear, respectful, timely, and accessible to all families — including those with different language backgrounds.
Create structured opportunities for families to engage with the school as active partners, not just recipients of information.
Respond promptly and professionally to parent concerns and use family feedback to improve department practice.
selfAssessment: I can ensure that all department communications with families are clear, professional, and accessible — including appropriate use of language and tone for diverse family communities.
I can design and facilitate a family engagement activity — such as a curriculum evening, student-led conference, or parent workshop — that involves families as active participants.
I can respond to a parent concern or complaint in a timely, professional, and solution-focused way.
I can use feedback from families — gathered through surveys, conversations, or meetings — to make at least one improvement to department practice.
I can identify which families in my area are not engaging with current outreach activities and design a targeted approach to better reach them.

activity.task: Design and facilitate a family engagement event for your department or year group. Collect feedback from participants and produce a short evaluation report.
activity.output: A family engagement event plan (1 page) and a post-event evaluation summary (1 page) covering: event description, number of families engaged, feedback received, and two or three actions to improve future engagement.
activity.evidence: Completed event plan and evaluation summary; evidence of family attendance or participation; feedback collected from families.

advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, you lead family engagement strategy across a programme or phase. You design and coordinate structured processes — including multi-language communications, family survey cycles, and whole-phase parent forums — that ensure all families can participate meaningfully in their children's education. You analyse family engagement data to identify patterns — such as which families are not participating and why — and design targeted, culturally responsive responses. You also support department heads to improve their family communication practices through coaching and shared frameworks. PSEL Standard 8 requires leaders to establish systems for meaningful family engagement that are equitable, sustainable, and built on genuine partnership. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 calls on leaders to build inclusive relationships with all stakeholders, recognising that diverse families bring different needs, languages, and expectations. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 includes community and family engagement as a principal-level responsibility. At this level, family engagement is a strategic function you lead with data, intention, and cultural sensitivity.
keyTakeaways: Design and coordinate phase-level family engagement processes that are equitable, multi-language, and built on genuine partnership.
Analyse family engagement data to identify which families are not participating and design culturally responsive outreach strategies.
Support department heads to improve their family communication practices and build their capacity for meaningful family engagement.
selfAssessment: I can design a phase-level family engagement strategy that includes multiple communication channels, languages, and formats to reach all family communities.
I can analyse family engagement data — such as event attendance, survey response rates, and complaint records — to identify patterns and design targeted responses.
I can facilitate a whole-phase parent forum or consultation process that gives families a genuine voice in school planning and decision-making.
I can support department heads to improve the quality and accessibility of their family communication through coaching and shared frameworks.
I can produce a family engagement review report for senior leadership that summarises data, identifies priorities, and recommends systemic improvements.

activity.task: Design and lead a phase-level family engagement initiative — such as a multilingual parent survey, a cross-departmental parent forum, or a targeted outreach programme for underrepresented families. Evaluate its effectiveness and produce a report.
activity.output: A phase-level family engagement review report (2–3 pages) covering: initiative design, data or feedback gathered, families reached, key findings, and recommendations for sustaining or scaling the approach.
activity.evidence: Completed family engagement review report; data or feedback gathered; evidence of families reached; record of actions taken in response to findings.

lead.reading: At the Lead/Mentor level, you design and steward the school's whole family engagement and parent communication system. You create the policies, frameworks, communication protocols, and feedback processes that shape how the school builds relationships with all family communities. You ensure that family engagement is equitable — actively working to include families who face barriers to participation, including those from lower socioeconomic backgrounds, those who speak minority languages, or those with limited experience of formal education systems. You mentor other leaders to develop their family engagement skills and evaluate the effectiveness of the school's approach over time. PSEL Standard 8 calls on lead leaders to build authentic, equitable, and sustained family engagement as a structural feature of school governance. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 requires the most effective leaders to create conditions where all families feel genuinely welcomed and valued. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 places responsibility for community engagement with the school principal. At this level, family engagement is not a programme — it is a culture you have built and actively sustain.
keyTakeaways: Design the school's whole family engagement policy and communication framework, ensuring equitable access for all family communities.
Evaluate the effectiveness of the school's family engagement approach over time and use findings to improve systems and culture.
Mentor other leaders to develop family engagement leadership skills and build a school-wide culture where all families feel genuinely welcomed and valued.
selfAssessment: I can design a whole-school family engagement policy and communication framework that defines principles, processes, channels, and equity commitments.
I can evaluate the effectiveness and equity of the school's family engagement approach over time using attendance data, survey results, and stakeholder feedback.

I can lead the development of a school communications strategy that is accessible to all family communities — including those with different language backgrounds, literacy levels, or cultural expectations.
I can mentor two or more leaders to develop their family engagement leadership skills using structured coaching and evidence-based conversations.
I can represent the school's family engagement commitments and practices to Cambridge accreditation bodies and Indonesian government representatives.

activity.task: Design or significantly revise the school's whole-school family engagement policy and communication framework. Conduct a long-term equity review of family engagement data and mentor two leaders in their family communication practice.
activity.output: A whole-school family engagement policy and communication framework document (4–6 pages) including: engagement principles, communication protocols, equity commitments, feedback mechanisms, and an annual review process. A one-page equity review summary with data from at least two years. Mentoring logs from two structured sessions.
activity.evidence: Final policy and framework; equity review summary; mentoring logs; record of board or senior leadership presentation.

practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, you build and maintain relationships with community partners and local stakeholders that support your team or department's work. Community partners include local businesses, cultural organisations, universities, government agencies, and community groups. These partnerships can enrich student learning — through work experience, guest speakers, community projects, or shared events — and strengthen the school's connection to its local context. At this level, you identify partnership opportunities relevant to your area, initiate and maintain these relationships professionally, and ensure partnerships deliver genuine benefit to students. PSEL Standard 8 calls on leaders to engage the wider community as a resource for student learning and school development. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 requires leaders to build productive relationships with external stakeholders that serve the school's mission. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies community engagement and partnership as a core managerial competency for school principals. At this stage, community partnerships are a structured part of your leadership — not occasional or accidental connections.
keyTakeaways: Identify community partnership opportunities relevant to your department and initiate these relationships professionally and purposefully.
Ensure community partnerships deliver genuine benefit to students — through enriched learning, real-world connections, or community service.
Maintain partnerships through regular communication, clear expectations, and mutual benefit for both the school and the community partner.
selfAssessment: I can identify at least two community organisations, businesses, or institutions whose work is relevant to my department and describe how a partnership with each could benefit students.
I can initiate a partnership relationship with a community organisation using a professional approach that clearly explains the school's goals and expectations.
I can design and lead a student learning activity — such as a community project, guest speaker visit, or work experience placement — that uses a community partnership to enrich the curriculum.
I can maintain a community partnership through regular communication, clear mutual expectations, and review of the partnership's benefit to students.
I can report on the impact of a community partnership to senior leadership, with evidence of student benefit and partner satisfaction.

activity.task: Establish or significantly strengthen one community partnership relevant to your department. Design and deliver one student learning activity using the partnership and produce an impact summary.
activity.output: A community partnership activity plan (1 page) and a post-activity impact summary (1 page) covering: partnership description, activity delivered, student outcomes, partner feedback, and recommended next steps.
activity.evidence: Completed plan and impact summary; evidence of student participation; record of communication with the community partner; feedback collected.

advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, you lead community partnership development across a programme or phase. You design a coordinated approach to community engagement that connects multiple departments to a shared set of stakeholders. You manage complex or high-value partnerships — such as relationships with universities, government bodies, or major employers — that extend beyond individual departments. You evaluate the effectiveness of community partnerships across your phase and ensure they are equitable — benefiting all students, not just those who are easiest to reach. PSEL Standard 8 requires leaders to engage the community strategically, building partnerships that are sustainable and serve the school's long-term development. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 calls on leaders to build and sustain meaningful relationships with a wide range of stakeholders. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 includes community partnership management as a principal-level responsibility. At this level, community partnerships are a strategic leadership function you coordinate with purpose and long-term vision.
keyTakeaways: Lead a coordinated approach to community partnerships across multiple departments, ensuring partnerships are strategic, equitable, and aligned to school priorities.
Manage complex or high-value partnerships — such as institutional relationships with universities or government bodies — with professionalism and long-term intent.
Evaluate the effectiveness and equity of community partnerships across your phase, ensuring all students benefit from community engagement opportunities.
selfAssessment: I can design a phase-level community engagement strategy that coordinates partnerships across multiple departments and aligns them to school improvement priorities.
I can manage a complex or high-value institutional partnership — such as with a university, government body, or major employer — with professional relationship management and clear mutual goals.
I can evaluate the effectiveness and equity of community partnerships across my phase, identifying which student groups benefit most and which may be underserved.
I can facilitate a cross-departmental partnership planning meeting that results in a shared calendar of community engagement activities for the academic year.

I can produce a community partnership impact report for senior leadership covering the range of partnerships active across my phase, their contribution to student outcomes, and recommendations for development.

activity.task: Design a phase-level community engagement strategy and manage at least one high-value institutional partnership for one academic year. Produce an annual community partnership impact report.
activity.output: A phase-level community engagement strategy (1–2 pages) and an annual community partnership impact report (2–3 pages) covering: partnerships active, activities delivered, student reach, equity analysis, key findings, and recommended actions for the following year.
activity.evidence: Completed strategy and impact report; evidence of partnership activities delivered; student participation data; record of at least one institutional partnership meeting.

lead.reading: At the Lead/Mentor level, you design and steward the school's whole community partnership and stakeholder engagement system. You create the policies, frameworks, and governance structures that guide how the school builds, maintains, and evaluates relationships with community partners at all levels. You lead strategic partnerships at the highest institutional level — representing the school with government agencies, foundations, professional bodies, and community leaders. You ensure that community engagement is equitable, sustainable, and genuinely connected to the school's educational mission. You mentor other leaders to develop their community partnership skills. PSEL Standard 8 calls on lead leaders to build and sustain a community engagement culture where the school is an active, contributing member of its wider community. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 requires the most effective leaders to develop and manage external stakeholder relationships that serve the school's long-term mission. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 places responsibility for institutional community engagement with the school principal. At this level, the school's reputation in and contribution to its community is a leadership priority you actively shape.
keyTakeaways: Design the school's whole community partnership policy and stakeholder engagement framework, including governance structures and evaluation processes.
Lead the school's most strategic institutional partnerships, representing the school at the highest levels of community engagement.
Mentor other leaders to develop community partnership leadership skills and build a school-wide culture of purposeful, equitable community engagement.
selfAssessment: I can design a whole-school community partnership policy and stakeholder engagement framework that defines principles, partnership tiers, governance expectations, and evaluation processes.

I can lead strategic institutional partnerships at the highest level — including with government agencies, foundations, professional bodies, or community leaders — with professionalism and long-term vision.
I can evaluate the whole-school community engagement programme annually, assessing its equity, impact, and alignment to school priorities, and presenting findings to the board.
I can mentor two or more leaders to develop their community partnership leadership skills using structured coaching and evidence-based review conversations.
I can represent the school's community engagement commitments and impact to Cambridge accreditation bodies, Indonesian government representatives, and wider stakeholders.

activity.task: Design or significantly revise the school's whole-school community partnership policy and stakeholder engagement framework. Lead an annual community engagement impact review and mentor two leaders in partnership development.
activity.output: A whole-school community partnership policy and stakeholder engagement framework document (4–6 pages) including: partnership principles, partnership tiers, governance expectations, evaluation framework, and annual review cycle. A one-page annual community engagement impact summary. Mentoring logs from two structured sessions.
activity.evidence: Final framework document; impact summary; mentoring logs; record of board or senior leadership presentation.

practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, you actively participate in Cambridge networks and represent your school externally in relevant professional communities. Cambridge provides a range of network opportunities — including regional professional development events, Cambridge teacher networks, examiner roles, and centre-to-centre collaboration. At this level, you engage with these opportunities purposefully — attending Cambridge events, connecting with teachers and leaders from other Cambridge schools, and bringing insights back to your own team. You also represent your school professionally in external contexts such as Cambridge regional meetings, professional conferences, or online communities of practice. PSEL Standard 8 calls on leaders to build the school's presence and credibility in the wider professional community. Cambridge School Leader Standard 3 requires leaders to engage with Cambridge networks and external evaluation processes as part of professional development. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies external professional engagement as a component of the school leader's social competency. At this stage, your participation in Cambridge networks strengthens both your own practice and your school's connections in the broader Cambridge community.
keyTakeaways: Engage purposefully with Cambridge networks — including events, teacher communities, and collaboration opportunities — and share insights with your team.
Represent your school professionally in external contexts such as Cambridge regional meetings, conferences, or online professional communities.
Use your participation in Cambridge networks to identify ideas and practices that can improve your department's work.
selfAssessment: I can describe the Cambridge professional network opportunities available to teachers and leaders at my school and explain how to access them.
I can attend and actively participate in at least one Cambridge network event or meeting per year, and share key insights with my team.
I can represent my school professionally in an external context — such as a Cambridge regional meeting or professional conference — by contributing ideas and describing my school's practice clearly.
I can identify at least one idea or practice from the Cambridge network that I have introduced or plan to introduce in my own department.
I can support a teacher in my team to access a Cambridge professional development opportunity — such as a Cambridge PDQ pathway, a Cambridge teacher network, or a Cambridge Examiner role.

activity.task: Attend at least one Cambridge network event or professional meeting and produce a written reflection and knowledge-sharing summary for your team.
activity.output: A written network engagement reflection and summary (1–2 pages) covering: event or meeting attended, key ideas encountered, relevance to your department, and at least one action you plan to take based on what you learned.
activity.duration: As events become available — aim for completion within one term
activity.evidence: Completed reflection and summary; evidence of attendance such as a registration confirmation or event programme; record of the team sharing session where insights were discussed.

advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, you lead the school's engagement with Cambridge networks and manage external professional representation at programme or phase level. You coordinate your phase's participation in Cambridge professional development events, examiner networks, and school-to-school collaboration opportunities. You represent your school at Cambridge regional forums, stakeholder meetings, and professional conferences — contributing your school's experience and learning from peers. You build relationships with Cambridge representatives and other Cambridge school leaders that strengthen the school's external profile and access to new ideas and opportunities. PSEL Standard 8 requires leaders to use external engagement strategically to build the school's professional reputation and access new resources and ideas. Cambridge School Leader Standard 3 calls on leaders to engage with Cambridge networks and external evaluation processes as a professional responsibility. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 identifies external and social competency as a required dimension of school principal leadership. At this level, your external engagement creates tangible benefits for your phase — bringing new thinking, strengthening partnerships, and raising the school's profile in the Cambridge community.
keyTakeaways: Coordinate phase-level participation in Cambridge professional development, examiner roles, and school-to-school collaboration opportunities.
Represent your school at Cambridge regional forums and stakeholder meetings, contributing your school's experience and building relationships with Cambridge peers.
Use external engagement strategically to bring new ideas, practices, and opportunities back to your phase.
selfAssessment: I can coordinate phase-level participation in Cambridge professional development events, examiner roles, and school-to-school collaboration, ensuring equitable access across departments.
I can represent my school at a Cambridge regional forum or stakeholder meeting by presenting the school's work clearly and contributing substantively to professional discussions.
I can build and maintain productive professional relationships with Cambridge representatives and leaders from other Cambridge schools.
I can use insights from Cambridge network engagement to design or improve professional development activities in my phase.
I can produce a Cambridge network engagement report for senior leadership covering participation across my phase, key insights gained, and recommended actions for the following year.

activity.task: Lead your phase's Cambridge network engagement for one academic year. Coordinate participation in events and collaboration opportunities and produce an annual engagement report.
activity.output: A phase-level Cambridge network engagement report (2–3 pages) covering: events and opportunities accessed, staff participation, key insights gathered, actions taken or planned, and recommendations for the following year.
activity.evidence: Completed network engagement report; evidence of events attended or roles held; staff participation records; record of at least one action taken in response to network insights.

lead.reading: At the Lead/Mentor level, you design and lead the school's whole Cambridge network engagement strategy and manage external professional representation at institutional level. You make strategic decisions about which Cambridge network opportunities — such as examiner roles, centre partnerships, research collaborations, or advisory group membership — best serve the school's long-term development. You represent the school at the most senior Cambridge forums and in formal accreditation or evaluation contexts. You also build the school's profile as a contributing member of the global Cambridge community — sharing expertise, hosting professional visitors, and contributing to Cambridge's own professional development activities. PSEL Standard 8 calls on lead leaders to establish the school as a visible, credible, and contributing member of the wider educational community. Cambridge School Leader Standard 3 requires the most effective leaders to engage with Cambridge as an active institutional partner — not just a centre that delivers programmes. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 places responsibility for external institutional representation with the school principal. At this level, the school's external profile is a strategic asset you actively develop.
keyTakeaways: Design the school's whole Cambridge network engagement strategy, making strategic decisions about which opportunities best serve long-term school development.
Represent the school at the most senior Cambridge forums and formal accreditation or evaluation contexts.
Build the school's profile as a contributing Cambridge community member by sharing expertise, hosting professional visitors, and participating in Cambridge's professional development activities.
selfAssessment: I can design a whole-school Cambridge network engagement strategy that identifies priority opportunities, assigns responsibilities, and sets targets for participation and impact.
I can represent the school at senior Cambridge forums, accreditation events, or external professional conferences with confidence, credibility, and institutional authority.
I can build the school's profile as a contributing Cambridge community member by hosting professional visits, sharing practice, or contributing to Cambridge professional development content.
I can mentor two or more leaders to develop their external professional representation skills using structured coaching and supported external engagement opportunities.
I can evaluate the school's Cambridge network engagement strategy annually and present findings and recommended adjustments to the board.

activity.task: Design or significantly revise the school's whole-school Cambridge network engagement strategy. Lead at least one high-profile external engagement activity and mentor two leaders in external professional representation.
activity.output: A whole-school Cambridge network engagement strategy document (3–5 pages) including: strategic rationale, priority network opportunities, participation targets, leadership responsibilities, and evaluation framework. A record of at least one high-profile external engagement activity. Mentoring logs from two structured sessions.
activity.evidence: Final strategy document; record of external engagement activity; mentoring logs; record of board or senior leadership presentation.

advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, you lead the development and management of university articulation and student pathway partnerships at programme or phase level. University articulation refers to formal or informal agreements between your school and universities — establishing how Cambridge qualifications are recognised for admission, credit transfer, or scholarship opportunities. Student pathway partnerships may also include connections with vocational training providers, international universities, or career guidance services. You map the typical student pathways from your programme — including destinations in Indonesia, the UK, Australia, or other countries — and ensure students are well prepared and supported in their transitions. PSEL Standard 8 calls on leaders to build partnerships that support students' transitions and long-term success. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 requires leaders to develop relationships that serve all students' futures — not just those going to the most competitive institutions. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 includes community and external stakeholder engagement as a principal-level leadership responsibility. At this level, you ensure that every student in your phase has a clear pathway and the support to pursue it.
keyTakeaways: Map the typical university and post-school pathways of students in your programme or phase and identify where support or partnerships are needed.
Build and maintain relationships with universities, scholarship bodies, and other post-school pathway providers that benefit students in your phase.
Ensure that all students — not just the highest achieving — receive guidance and support for their post-school transitions.
selfAssessment: I can map the main university and post-school pathways of students in my programme or phase, including typical destinations and common challenges in the transition process.
I can identify at least three university or post-school pathway partners that would benefit students in my phase and describe what each partnership could provide.
I can organise and lead a university or pathway guidance activity — such as a university fair, scholarship information session, or career guidance workshop — for students in my phase.
I can ensure that students from all backgrounds — including those who may be first-generation university applicants — receive appropriate guidance and encouragement for post-school transitions.
I can produce a student pathway partnership report for senior leadership covering current partnerships, student destination data, equity of access, and recommended development priorities.

activity.task: Map student post-school pathways in your programme or phase. Design and deliver at least one university or pathway guidance activity. Produce a student pathway partnership development plan.
activity.output: A student pathway partnership development plan (2–3 pages) covering: current pathway data summary, student destination analysis, existing partnerships, gaps identified, new partnerships proposed, and an equity assessment of current pathway support.
activity.evidence: Completed partnership development plan; evidence of guidance activity delivered; student destination data used; record of communication with at least two external pathway partners.

lead.reading: At the Lead/Mentor level, you design and steward the school's whole university articulation and student pathway partnership system. You create the policies, processes, and partnership portfolio that ensure all students leave the school with a clear and supported pathway to their next destination. You build formal articulation agreements with universities — particularly those that recognise Cambridge qualifications for admission or credit — and represent the school in high-level meetings with university partners, scholarship bodies, and ministry representatives. You ensure that the school's guidance programme is equitable — giving students from all socioeconomic backgrounds and family education levels the support they need. PSEL Standard 8 calls on lead leaders to build partnerships that serve every student's long-term success and to ensure these partnerships are equitable and sustainable. Cambridge School Leader Standard 1 requires the most effective leaders to build institutional relationships that open genuine opportunities for all students. Permendiknas No. 13/2007 places responsibility for community and institutional partnership governance with the school principal. At this level, every student's future is a whole-school leadership commitment you have built systems to support.
keyTakeaways: Design the school's whole university articulation and student pathway partnership system, including partnership policy, guidance programme, and equity framework.
Build and maintain formal articulation agreements with universities and represent the school at the most senior level with university and scholarship partners.
Ensure the school's pathway guidance programme is equitable — actively supporting students from all backgrounds to access appropriate post-school opportunities.
selfAssessment: I can design a whole-school university articulation and student pathway partnership policy that defines the school's approach to university engagement, scholarship support, and career guidance.
I can build and maintain formal articulation agreements with universities — particularly those that recognise Cambridge qualifications — and represent the school in senior-level partnership meetings.

I can design an equitable school-wide guidance programme that ensures all students — including those from disadvantaged backgrounds or first-generation university families — receive structured pathway support.
I can mentor two or more leaders to develop their university articulation and student pathway partnership skills using structured coaching and evidence-based conversations.
I can evaluate the school's university articulation and pathway partnership system annually using student destination data, equity analysis, and partner feedback, and present findings to the board.

activity.task: Design or significantly revise the school's whole-school university articulation and student pathway partnership system. Conduct an equity review of student destinations and mentor two leaders in pathway partnership development.
activity.output: A whole-school university articulation and student pathway partnership policy and framework document (4–6 pages) including: partnership principles, university articulation process, guidance programme structure, equity commitments, and annual review process. A one-page equity review of student destinations using data from at least two years. Mentoring logs from two structured sessions.
activity.evidence: Final policy and framework document; student destination equity review; mentoring logs; record of board or senior leadership presentation; evidence of at least one formal university articulation agreement or partnership developed.
